q: A 62-year-old male presents with 2 hours of crushing chest pain, ST elevation in V1-V4, and troponin rising. He receives aspirin 325mg and is taken for emergent PCI. Which P2Y12 inhibitor is preferred in this setting?
opts: A. Clopidogrel 300mg load
B. Ticagrelor 180mg load
C. Prasugrel 60mg load
D. Vorapaxar 2.5mg
exp: Ticagrelor 180mg load is preferred in STEMI for PCI per ACC/AHA guidelines due to faster, more predictable platelet inhibition compared to clopidogrel. Prasugrel is also acceptable but has higher bleeding risk; it is contraindicated in prior stroke/TIA, age >75, or weight <60kg. Vorapaxar is a PAR-1 antagonist not used in acute ACS.

q: A 58-year-old woman with HFrEF (EF 30%) is already on metoprolol succinate and enalapril. Her NYHA class remains II. Which additional therapy has proven mortality benefit?
opts: A. Amlodipine
B. Spironolactone
C. Hydralazine monotherapy
D. Digoxin
exp: Spironolactone (MRA) reduces mortality in HFrEF in patients with NYHA II-IV already on ACEi/ARB and beta-blocker (RALES, EMPHASIS-HF trials). Amlodipine is hemodynamically neutral but has no mortality benefit. Hydralazine/nitrate combo has benefit in Black patients intolerant of ACEi/ARB. Digoxin reduces hospitalizations but not mortality.

q: A patient with HFrEF (EF 25%) is on optimal medical therapy. The ARNI sacubitril/valsartan is being considered. The patient takes lisinopril 10mg daily. What is the required washout period before switching to sacubitril/valsartan?
opts: A. 12 hours
B. 24 hours
C. 36 hours
D. 72 hours
exp: A 36-hour washout from ACE inhibitors is required before starting sacubitril/valsartan to prevent angioedema (both inhibit bradykinin degradation). No washout needed when switching from ARB to ARNI. The PARADIGM-HF trial showed sacubitril/valsartan reduced CV death and HF hospitalization vs enalapril.

q: A 70-year-old man with stable angina has CCS class II symptoms on aspirin and a beta-blocker. His LDL is 105 mg/dL. His 10-year ASCVD risk is 22%. What is the most appropriate lipid-lowering strategy?
opts: A. Moderate-intensity statin
B. High-intensity statin
C. Ezetimibe alone
D. No additional therapy needed
exp: Patients with established ASCVD (stable angina = clinical ASCVD) should receive high-intensity statin therapy targeting >50% LDL reduction (ACC/AHA 2018). An LDL of 105 is above the <70 mg/dL goal for very high-risk patients. High-intensity statins (atorvastatin 40-80mg, rosuvastatin 20-40mg) are first-line. Ezetimibe is add-on if LDL goal not met on max statin.

q: A patient with AFib and CHA2DS2-VASc score of 3 (HTN, age 68, DM) is started on rivaroxaban 20mg daily. Six months later, her CrCl drops to 38 mL/min. What action is needed?
opts: A. No change, continue 20mg daily
B. Switch to rivaroxaban 15mg daily with evening meal
C. Discontinue rivaroxaban, start aspirin
D. Switch to warfarin
exp: Rivaroxaban requires dose reduction to 15mg daily with the evening meal when CrCl falls to 15-50 mL/min in AFib. The standard 20mg dose is for CrCl >50 mL/min. Aspirin alone is inferior for stroke prevention in AFib. Warfarin is an alternative but requires INR monitoring.

q: A 55-year-old man with HTN, DM, and a recent TIA (3 weeks ago) is in AFib. His CHA2DS2-VASc is 5. He needs anticoagulation. Which DOAC is contraindicated?
opts: A. Apixaban
B. Rivaroxaban
C. Prasugrel
D. Dabigatran
exp: Prasugrel is contraindicated in patients with prior TIA or stroke — this is a black box warning. It is a P2Y12 inhibitor (antiplatelet), not an anticoagulant. For AFib anticoagulation, apixaban, rivaroxaban, or dabigatran are all appropriate options. Apixaban is often preferred due to reduced bleeding risk (ARISTOTLE trial).

q: A patient on warfarin for mechanical heart valve has an INR of 7.2 with no active bleeding. Which management is most appropriate?
opts: A. Hold warfarin 1-2 doses, recheck INR
B. Administer vitamin K 10mg IV
C. Administer 4-factor PCC immediately
D. Administer idarucizumab
exp: For supratherapeutic INR (5-10) without bleeding, holding 1-2 warfarin doses and rechecking INR is appropriate. Low-dose oral vitamin K (1-2.5mg) can be added for faster reversal. IV vitamin K 10mg is reserved for major bleeding. 4F-PCC is for life-threatening bleeding. Idarucizumab reverses dabigatran only, not warfarin.

q: A 72-year-old woman with HTN has a blood pressure of 158/92 mmHg on lisinopril 10mg daily. She also has stage 3 CKD with proteinuria (urine albumin-creatinine ratio 450 mg/g). What is the best next step?
opts: A. Add amlodipine
B. Add hydrochlorothiazide
C. Increase lisinopril and add amlodipine
D. Switch to losartan
exp: In CKD with proteinuria, maximizing RAS blockade (increasing ACEi dose) is appropriate. Adding amlodipine (CCB) is an effective add-on for BP control with proven outcomes. Adding a thiazide is less effective when CrCl <30. Switching to an ARB provides no added benefit over ACEi unless ACEi is not tolerated. Combination ACEi+ARB is NOT recommended due to increased AKI/hyperkalemia risk.

q: A patient in the ICU develops new atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (HR 145 bpm) and BP 88/60 mmHg. What is the first-line treatment?
opts: A. IV diltiazem
B. IV metoprolol
C. IV amiodarone
D. Synchronized cardioversion
exp: Hemodynamically unstable AFib with rapid ventricular response requires immediate synchronized DC cardioversion. Pharmacologic rate control (diltiazem, metoprolol) and rhythm control agents (amiodarone) are used in stable patients. IV diltiazem and non-dihydropyridine CCBs are negative inotropes and should be avoided in cardiogenic shock.

q: A patient is started on sacubitril/valsartan for HFrEF. Two weeks later she develops angioedema. What is the most appropriate management?
opts: A. Reduce dose and observe
B. Discontinue sacubitril/valsartan and switch to valsartan alone
C. Discontinue and switch to an ACE inhibitor
D. Give epinephrine and resume at lower dose
exp: Sacubitril/valsartan-associated angioedema requires discontinuation. The sacubitril component inhibits neprilysin, which degrades bradykinin — this is the mechanism of angioedema. Switching to an ARB alone (without neprilysin inhibition) is appropriate. An ACE inhibitor is contraindicated as it also inhibits ACE (bradykinin degradation), worsening angioedema risk.

q: A 45-year-old man with no comorbidities presents with community-acquired pneumonia. He is treated as an outpatient. His chest X-ray shows a left lower lobe infiltrate. He has no drug allergies. What is the preferred treatment?
opts: A. Azithromycin 500mg day 1, then 250mg days 2-5
B. Amoxicillin 1g TID for 5 days
C. Levofloxacin 750mg daily for 5 days
D. Doxycycline 100mg BID for 5 days
exp: ATS/IDSA 2019 guidelines recommend amoxicillin as first-line monotherapy for outpatient CAP in patients without comorbidities. Doxycycline or a macrolide (azithromycin) are alternatives if amoxicillin cannot be used. Respiratory fluoroquinolones (levofloxacin) are preferred for patients with comorbidities or risk for drug-resistant organisms.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: A hospitalized patient develops C. difficile infection (CDI) with WBC 18k, creatinine 1.9 (baseline 0.9), and 8 loose stools/day. Stool toxin PCR is positive. How should this be classified and treated?
opts: A. Non-severe CDI: oral metronidazole
B. Non-severe CDI: oral vancomycin 125mg QID
C. Severe CDI: oral vancomycin 125mg QID
D. Severe CDI: IV metronidazole + oral vancomycin
exp: Severe CDI is defined by WBC >15k OR SCr >1.5 mg/dL. IDSA 2017 guidelines recommend oral vancomycin 125mg QID or fidaxomicin 200mg BID for severe CDI. Metronidazole is no longer recommended for severe CDI due to inferior outcomes. IV metronidazole can be added for ileus but oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin should be the backbone.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: A patient with MRSA bacteremia is on vancomycin. Day 3 blood cultures remain positive. Vancomycin MIC is 2 mg/L. What is the most appropriate change?
opts: A. Increase vancomycin dose to achieve trough 20-25
B. Switch to daptomycin 8-10 mg/kg/day
C. Add rifampin to vancomycin
D. Switch to linezolid
exp: Persistent MRSA bacteremia despite vancomycin with MIC 2 mg/L (heteroresistance or reduced susceptibility) warrants switching to daptomycin 8-10 mg/kg/day (high-dose for bacteremia/endocarditis). The 2020 ASHP/IDSA vancomycin guidelines state that vancomycin should not be used when MRSA MIC >2 (AUC/MIC cannot reach 400-600). Rifampin is not recommended as routine add-on for bacteremia.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: A 28-year-old woman with no prior antibiotic exposure presents with uncomplicated UTI (dysuria, frequency, no fever). She has a sulfa allergy (rash). What is the best first-line treatment?
opts: A. TMP-SMX DS BID x 3 days
B. Nitrofurantoin 100mg ER BID x 5 days
C. Ciprofloxacin 250mg BID x 3 days
D. Amoxicillin 500mg TID x 7 days
exp: For uncomplicated UTI, nitrofurantoin macrocrystals (100mg ER BID x 5 days) is a preferred first-line agent (IDSA guidelines). TMP-SMX is also first-line but is contraindicated with sulfa allergy. Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin) are effective but are reserved for when first-line agents cannot be used to preserve the drug class. Amoxicillin has high E. coli resistance rates.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: A patient with community-acquired pneumonia requiring ICU admission has no obvious risk factors for Pseudomonas. Which antibiotic regimen is recommended?
opts: A. Azithromycin monotherapy
B. Ceftriaxone + azithromycin or a respiratory FQ alone
C. Piperacillin-tazobactam + vancomycin
D. Meropenem + azithromycin
exp: Severe CAP requiring ICU admission is treated with a beta-lactam (ceftriaxone or ampicillin-sulbactam) PLUS a macrolide OR a respiratory fluoroquinolone alone. Anti-pseudomonal coverage (pip-tazo, meropenem) is only added if there are specific risk factors (structural lung disease, prior Pseudomonas isolation, recent antibiotics, hospitalization in last 90 days).
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: A patient with bacterial meningitis has Gram-positive diplococci on CSF Gram stain. She is started on empiric ceftriaxone and vancomycin. Culture later confirms S. pneumoniae with penicillin MIC of 0.03 (sensitive). What is the best definitive therapy?
opts: A. Continue vancomycin alone
B. Discontinue ceftriaxone; continue vancomycin
C. Continue ceftriaxone; discontinue vancomycin
D. Switch to ampicillin
exp: For penicillin-sensitive S. pneumoniae meningitis (MIC <0.06), ceftriaxone alone is the treatment of choice. Vancomycin can be discontinued once susceptibilities confirm sensitivity. High-dose penicillin G or ampicillin are alternatives. Dexamethasone adjunctive therapy (0.15 mg/kg q6h x 4 days, first dose before or with antibiotics) reduces neurologic complications.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: A patient with HIV (CD4 count 45) develops fever, headache, and altered mental status. India ink stain of CSF is positive. What is the treatment of choice for acute induction?
opts: A. Fluconazole 800mg daily
B. Amphotericin B deoxycholate + 5-flucytosine
C. Liposomal amphotericin B + fluconazole
D. Voriconazole 6mg/kg q12h x 2 doses
exp: Cryptococcal meningitis induction therapy per IDSA guidelines: amphotericin B (deoxycholate or liposomal) PLUS flucytosine (5-FC) 100 mg/kg/day divided QID for 2 weeks, followed by consolidation with fluconazole 400mg daily. Liposomal amphotericin B is preferred in resource-rich settings due to less nephrotoxicity. Fluconazole monotherapy is inferior for induction.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: A patient with septic shock is started on empiric antibiotics. Blood cultures return Enterococcus faecalis (VSE) in 2/2 bottles. Which antibiotic is most appropriate for definitive therapy?
opts: A. Vancomycin
B. Linezolid
C. Ampicillin
D. Daptomycin
exp: Ampicillin is the drug of choice for vancomycin-sensitive Enterococcus (VSE) faecalis bacteremia. E. faecalis is intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins. Vancomycin is acceptable but inferior to ampicillin for susceptible strains. For VRE (vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus), linezolid or daptomycin are used.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: A 35-year-old man is diagnosed with latent TB infection (positive IGRA, negative CXR, no symptoms). He has no prior TB treatment. What is the preferred preventive therapy?
opts: A. INH 300mg daily x 9 months
B. INH 300mg daily x 6 months
C. Rifapentine + INH weekly x 12 weeks (3HP)
D. Rifampin 600mg daily x 4 months
exp: The preferred LTBI regimens are shorter course: 3HP (rifapentine + INH weekly x 12 weeks) or 4R (rifampin daily x 4 months), which have improved completion rates over 9H (INH x 9 months). Both 3HP and 4R are CDC/IDSA preferred. INH x 6 months (6H) is an alternative but less effective than 9H. 9H remains acceptable.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: A patient hospitalized for 8 days develops a new fever, purulent sputum, and CXR infiltrate. His endotracheal aspirate grows Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Which antibiotic combination is appropriate for empiric VAP with Pseudomonas?
opts: A. Ceftriaxone + metronidazole
B. Cefepime + tobramycin
C. Vancomycin + metronidazole
D. TMP-SMX + azithromycin
exp: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) with high risk of multidrug-resistant (MDR) organisms requires anti-pseudomonal coverage. Cefepime (or piperacillin-tazobactam, meropenem) plus an aminoglycoside (tobramycin, amikacin) or fluoroquinolone provides dual Pseudomonas coverage. MRSA coverage (vancomycin or linezolid) is added if risk factors for MRSA are present. Ceftriaxone has no Pseudomonas coverage.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: A 65-year-old diabetic patient has a foot ulcer probing to bone. MRI confirms osteomyelitis. MRSA is cultured from the bone biopsy. Duration of IV antibiotic therapy for osteomyelitis is typically:
opts: A. 7-10 days
B. 2-3 weeks
C. 4-6 weeks
D. 3-6 months
exp: Hematogenous or contiguous osteomyelitis typically requires 4-6 weeks of antibiotic therapy (total). For MRSA osteomyelitis, vancomycin (target AUC 400-600) or daptomycin are first-line IV options. Oral step-down with linezolid, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, or clindamycin (if susceptible) can be considered for completion of therapy. Duration may be shorter with adequate surgical debridement.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: A patient with Candida albicans fungemia (bloodstream infection) is hemodynamically stable. Which antifungal is preferred for initial treatment?
opts: A. Fluconazole 800mg load then 400mg daily
B. An echinocandin (caspofungin, micafungin, or anidulafungin)
C. Voriconazole 6mg/kg q12h x 2 doses
D. Amphotericin B liposomal
exp: IDSA 2016 guidelines recommend an echinocandin as first-line for candidemia, particularly in critically ill patients or when fluconazole resistance is possible. Fluconazole can be used for non-critically ill patients with susceptible Candida and no recent azole exposure. All central venous catheters should be removed if possible. Treat for 14 days after last positive blood culture.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: A patient in septic shock requires vasopressor therapy. Norepinephrine 0.25 mcg/kg/min is running. His MAP is 58 mmHg (target >65). What is the most appropriate next step?
opts: A. Add dopamine
B. Add vasopressin 0.03-0.04 units/min
C. Switch to phenylephrine
D. Switch to epinephrine
exp: Vasopressin 0.03-0.04 units/min is the recommended second-line vasopressor added to norepinephrine in septic shock (Surviving Sepsis Campaign). Adding vasopressin has a corticosteroid-sparing effect and may allow reduction in norepinephrine dose. Dopamine has more arrhythmias than norepinephrine. Phenylephrine (pure alpha-1) should be avoided in low cardiac output states. Epinephrine is third-line.

q: A mechanically ventilated patient has ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 = 140, bilateral infiltrates, PCWP normal). Current settings: TV 8 mL/kg IBW, FiO2 0.7, PEEP 8. Plateau pressure is 34 cmH2O. What change is indicated?
opts: A. Decrease PEEP to 5
B. Reduce tidal volume to 6 mL/kg IBW
C. Increase FiO2 to 1.0
D. Switch to pressure control ventilation
exp: ARDSNet protocol mandates tidal volume of 6 mL/kg IBW and plateau pressure target ≤30 cmH2O. This patient has TV of 8 mL/kg and Pplat 34 — both above targets. Reducing TV to 6 mL/kg will lower Pplat and reduce ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI). PEEP should be increased (not decreased) in moderate-severe ARDS to recruit alveoli.

q: A patient in the ICU on mechanical ventilation has a CAM-ICU positive assessment. He is agitated (RASS +2) and pulling at his ETT. He is currently on fentanyl infusion. What is the first-line pharmacologic approach for ICU delirium management?
opts: A. Lorazepam infusion
B. Haloperidol 5mg IV
C. Quetiapine 50mg BID
D. Dexmedetomidine infusion
exp: Per PADIS 2018 guidelines, dexmedetomidine is preferred over benzodiazepines for ICU sedation and is associated with reduced delirium duration. Haloperidol and quetiapine do not reduce delirium duration or ICU mortality (MIND-USA trial) but may be used for comfort/symptoms. Lorazepam/benzodiazepines are associated with worsening delirium and should be avoided unless alcohol withdrawal or seizures.

q: An ICU patient develops fever (39.8°C), rigors, and hypotension 2 hours after blood transfusion. His blood type was verified. Hemoglobin has not dropped. What is the most likely reaction?
opts: A. Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction
B. Febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reaction
C. Transfusion-related acute lung injury
D. Anaphylactic reaction
exp: Febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reaction (FNHTR) presents with fever, chills, and rigors during or within 4 hours of transfusion, without hemolysis. It is caused by cytokines from donor leukocytes. Management: stop transfusion, administer acetaminophen. Acute hemolytic reactions (type mismatch) cause hemoglobin drop, flank pain, hemoglobinuria. TRALI presents with hypoxia and bilateral infiltrates within 6 hours.

q: A 68-year-old man weighs 110 kg (IBW 72 kg) and has a SCr of 1.4 mg/dL. What is the most accurate weight to use for Cockcroft-Gault in an obese patient?
opts: A. Actual body weight (110 kg)
B. Ideal body weight (72 kg)
C. Adjusted body weight (72 + 0.4×38 = 87.2 kg)
D. Lean body weight (68 kg)
exp: For obese patients (ABW > 30% above IBW), adjusted body weight (AdjBW = IBW + 0.4 × [TBW - IBW]) is used in the Cockcroft-Gault equation to avoid overestimating CrCl. Using actual body weight overestimates renal function. IBW alone underestimates it. AdjBW = 72 + 0.4(110-72) = 72 + 15.2 = 87.2 kg.

q: A patient receives vancomycin 1500mg q12h. A pre-dose (trough) level at steady state is 12 mcg/mL. A peak drawn 1 hour after infusion end is 32 mcg/mL. Using the two-level approach, what PK parameter is primarily used for AUC estimation?
opts: A. Trough level only
B. Peak and trough for Bayesian AUC estimation
C. Volume of distribution
D. Half-life calculation from single level
exp: The 2020 ASHP/IDSA/SIDP vancomycin guidelines recommend AUC-guided monitoring (target AUC/MIC 400-600 mg·h/L for MRSA with MIC ≤1). Two-level Bayesian estimation (using peak and trough) is the preferred method for AUC calculation, as trough-only monitoring is unreliable. One-point Bayesian estimation is an acceptable alternative.

q: A patient with a seizure disorder is on phenytoin 300mg daily. Her albumin is 2.2 g/dL (normal 4.0). Her measured phenytoin level is 8 mcg/mL. What is the corrected (true free equivalent) phenytoin level?
opts: A. 8 mcg/mL — no correction needed
C. Corrected = 8 × (4.0/2.2) = 14.5 mcg/mL
D. Corrected = 8 / (2.2/4.0) = 14.5 mcg/mL
exp: Winter-Tozer equation for hypoalbuminemia: Corrected phenytoin = Measured / [0.2 × (albumin) + 0.1]. With albumin 2.2: 8 / [0.2(2.2) + 0.1] = 8 / [0.44 + 0.1] = 8 / 0.54 = 14.8 mcg/mL. This indicates the free drug level is higher than it appears. The therapeutic range for total phenytoin is 10-20 mcg/mL. A measured level of 8 may appear subtherapeutic but the corrected level is therapeutic.

q: A drug has a volume of distribution (Vd) of 70L and a clearance (CL) of 5 L/h. What is the approximate half-life?
opts: A. 4.8 hours
B. 9.7 hours
C. 14 hours
D. 35 hours
exp: Half-life = 0.693 × Vd / CL = 0.693 × 70 / 5 = 9.7 hours. Steady state is reached in approximately 4-5 half-lives = 39-49 hours. Loading doses are used when immediate target concentrations are needed without waiting for steady state.

q: An aminoglycoside (gentamicin) is dosed using extended-interval dosing (once daily). Which PK/PD target is most important for efficacy?
opts: A. Time above MIC (T>MIC)
B. Trough level <1 mcg/mL
C. Cmax/MIC ratio ≥8-10
D. AUC/MIC 400-600
exp: Aminoglycosides are concentration-dependent antibiotics. Their primary PK/PD target for efficacy is Cmax/MIC ≥8-10. Extended-interval dosing (once daily) maximizes the Cmax/MIC ratio while allowing drug-free time to minimize nephrotoxicity. Trough <1 mcg/mL is monitored to reduce nephrotoxicity risk, not for efficacy. T>MIC is the target for beta-lactams. AUC/MIC is the target for vancomycin.

q: A patient requires a loading dose of amiodarone IV for new-onset AFib. The desired plasma concentration (Cp) is 2 mcg/mL, Vd is 60 L/kg, weight is 70 kg, and bioavailability is 1 (IV). What is the loading dose?
opts: A. 140 mg
B. 840 mg
exp: Loading dose = (Vd × Cp) / F = (60 L/kg × 70 kg × 2 mcg/mL) / 1 = 4200 L × 2 mcg/mL = 8400 mg. Amiodarone has a very large Vd (60 L/kg) due to extensive tissue binding, which is why loading doses are large (typically 150 mg IV bolus followed by infusions, given over hours/days to avoid toxicity despite the large theoretical dose).

q: A 58-year-old man with T2DM, HTN, and a recent MI (6 months ago) has A1c 8.4% on metformin 1000mg BID. His eGFR is 68 mL/min/1.73m2. Which add-on therapy is most appropriate?
opts: A. Glipizide 5mg daily
B. Sitagliptin 100mg daily
C. Semaglutide 0.5mg weekly
D. Pioglitazone 15mg daily
exp: For T2DM patients with established ASCVD (MI), GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide) or SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, canagliflozin, dapagliflozin) with proven CV benefit are preferred add-ons per ADA guidelines. Semaglutide (SUSTAIN-6) and liraglutide (LEADER) have demonstrated CV mortality reduction. Sulfonylureas (glipizide) increase hypoglycemia risk. Pioglitazone causes fluid retention/HF. Sitagliptin is weight-neutral with no CV benefit.

q: A 72-year-old patient with T2DM, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 35%), and eGFR 55 mL/min is on metformin and linagliptin. What is the best evidence-based add-on therapy?
opts: A. Glargine insulin
B. Dapagliflozin 10mg daily
C. Exenatide 5mcg BID
D. Canagliflozin 100mg daily
exp: SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) are the preferred agents in T2DM with HFrEF — DAPA-HF showed dapagliflozin reduces HF hospitalization and CV death in HFrEF regardless of diabetes status. Empagliflozin is also approved (EMPEROR-Reduced). Exenatide (GLP-1 RA) has not shown HF benefit and may worsen HF. Canagliflozin showed CV benefit (CANVAS) but dapagliflozin has specific HF indication.

q: A patient is brought to the ER with blood glucose of 620 mg/dL, serum osmolality 345 mOsm/kg, altered mental status, and no ketonemia. Arterial pH is 7.38. What is the diagnosis and initial IV fluid of choice?
opts: A. DKA; 0.9% NaCl
B. HHS; 0.9% NaCl
C. HHS; 0.45% NaCl
D. DKA; 0.45% NaCl
exp: This is hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS): glucose >600, serum osmolality >320, no significant ketosis, normal pH. Initial fluid resuscitation uses 0.9% NaCl (normal saline) to restore intravascular volume, then 0.45% NaCl after hemodynamic stability to correct free water deficit. Insulin is held until glucose is dropping and potassium ≥3.5. HHS has much higher mortality than DKA.

q: A patient on amiodarone for 2 years presents with fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, and constipation. TSH is 15 mIU/L (normal 0.4-4.0). Free T4 is low. What is the diagnosis and treatment?
opts: A. Amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis; methimazole
B. Amiodarone-induced hypothyroidism; levothyroxine
C. Subacute thyroiditis; NSAIDs
D. Hashimoto thyroiditis; no treatment yet
exp: Amiodarone (37% iodine by weight) commonly causes hypothyroidism (especially in iodine-sufficient regions) via the Wolff-Chaikoff effect. Elevated TSH + low free T4 = hypothyroidism. Treatment is levothyroxine while continuing amiodarone if needed for arrhythmia control. Amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis (AIT) presents with low TSH and elevated thyroid hormones — treated with methimazole +/- prednisone.

q: A patient with type 1 DM is admitted for DKA. Initial K+ is 3.2 mEq/L. When should insulin be started?
opts: A. Immediately with the first bag of IV fluids
B. After K+ is replaced to ≥3.5 mEq/L
C. After glucose falls below 250 mg/dL
D. After bicarb normalizes above 15
exp: Insulin drives K+ into cells and can worsen hypokalemia, causing life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias. Per ADA DKA protocol, insulin should NOT be started until K+ ≥3.5 mEq/L. Replace potassium IV (20-40 mEq/h) until K+ ≥3.5, then start insulin. Glucose cutoff of 250 mg/dL is when dextrose is added to IV fluids (not when to start insulin). Bicarb is rarely needed (only if pH <6.9).

q: A 55-year-old woman with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 22) has K+ 6.2 mEq/L, no ECG changes, and is asymptomatic. Which treatment is appropriate as a first step?
opts: A. Emergent dialysis
B. Kayexalate (sodium polystyrene sulfonate)
C. Patiromer 8.4g daily
D. IV calcium gluconate
exp: For chronic hyperkalemia in CKD without ECG changes, patiromer (a potassium binder) is appropriate for outpatient management. Patiromer is preferred over Kayexalate (sodium polystyrene sulfonate) due to better safety profile and no sorbitol-related GI complications. IV calcium gluconate is for acute hyperkalemia with ECG changes (membrane stabilization). Dialysis is reserved for severe, refractory, or dialysis-dependent patients.

q: A patient with ESRD on hemodialysis has AFib. Anticoagulation is needed (CHA2DS2-VASc = 4). Which DOAC has the most data supporting use in dialysis patients?
opts: A. Rivaroxaban 15mg daily
B. Dabigatran 75mg BID
C. Apixaban 5mg BID (or 2.5mg BID if qualifying criteria met)
D. Edoxaban 30mg daily
exp: Apixaban has the most evidence in ESRD/dialysis, as it has the least renal elimination (~27%). Several observational studies and the RENAL-AF trial (though stopped early) support apixaban use in dialysis patients. Dabigatran is 80% renally eliminated and is contraindicated in dialysis. Rivaroxaban requires CrCl >15 for AFib dosing. Edoxaban requires CrCl >15. Warfarin remains an option but is associated with calciphylaxis and vascular calcification in dialysis.

q: A patient presents with AKI (SCr rising from 0.9 to 2.8 mg/dL over 48 hours) after starting ibuprofen for knee pain. Urine sodium is <20 mEq/L, urine osmolality is >500 mOsm/kg, and fractional excretion of sodium (FeNa) is 0.4%. What type of AKI is this?
opts: A. Intrinsic AKI (acute tubular necrosis)
B. Postrenal AKI
C. Prerenal AKI
D. Contrast-induced nephropathy
exp: FeNa <1% with concentrated urine (Uosm >500) and low urine Na (<20) indicates prerenal AKI — the kidneys are intact and avidly retaining sodium. NSAIDs cause prerenal AKI by inhibiting prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriolar dilation, reducing GFR. Treatment: discontinue NSAID, IV fluids. Intrinsic AKI (ATN) has FeNa >2%, isosthenuria, muddy brown casts.

q: A patient with CKD stage 3b and diabetes has urine ACR of 800 mg/g. Current meds include metformin and lisinopril. What additional therapy has evidence to reduce progression of diabetic kidney disease?
opts: A. Amlodipine
B. Finerenone
C. Hydralazine
D. Allopurinol
exp: Finerenone (a non-steroidal MRA) is FDA-approved for CKD in T2DM based on the FIDELIO-DKD and FIGARO-DKD trials, showing reduced CKD progression and CV events. SGLT2 inhibitors (canagliflozin — CREDENCE; dapagliflozin — DAPA-CKD) also have strong evidence for DKD progression. The combination of ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i + finerenone represents the optimal cardiorenal protection regimen in DKD.

q: A 52-year-old woman is diagnosed with H. pylori-positive peptic ulcer disease. She has no penicillin allergy and has not received prior clarithromycin. Which regimen is recommended as first-line?
opts: A. Bismuth quadruple therapy x 14 days
B. Clarithromycin-based triple therapy (PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin) x 14 days
C. Levofloxacin triple therapy x 10 days
D. PPI monotherapy x 8 weeks
exp: Clarithromycin-based triple therapy (PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin) x 14 days is first-line in regions with low clarithromycin resistance (<15%) and no prior macrolide use. In areas with high resistance or prior macrolide exposure, bismuth quadruple therapy is preferred. Confirmation of eradication should be done ≥4 weeks after therapy completion with urea breath test or fecal antigen.

q: A patient with cirrhosis develops confusion, asterixis, and elevated ammonia. He is currently on lactulose. His current medications include propranolol 20mg BID, furosemide 40mg daily, and spironolactone 100mg daily. What is the most appropriate treatment for acute hepatic encephalopathy?
opts: A. Increase lactulose dose to achieve 2-3 soft stools/day
B. Add rifaximin 550mg BID
C. Start neomycin 1g TID
D. Start oral zinc supplementation only
exp: Acute hepatic encephalopathy treatment: titrate lactulose to achieve 2-3 soft bowel movements per day (pH lowering converts NH3 to NH4+ which is trapped in the gut). Rifaximin 550mg BID is added to lactulose for secondary prevention of recurrent HE (not first-line for acute). Neomycin is avoided due to nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity. Zinc supplementation is adjunctive. Identify and treat precipitating factors (GI bleed, infection, electrolyte disturbance, constipation).

q: A patient with IBD (Crohn's disease) is on infliximab maintenance therapy. He develops fever, productive cough, and night sweats. Chest X-ray shows an upper lobe cavitary lesion. What is the most likely diagnosis and required action before infliximab continuation?
opts: A. CAP from S. pneumoniae; continue infliximab
B. Active pulmonary tuberculosis; hold infliximab and treat TB fully
C. Aspergillosis; hold infliximab and start voriconazole
D. Histoplasmosis; hold infliximab and start itraconazole
exp: Cavitary upper lobe lesion in an immunosuppressed patient on anti-TNF therapy (infliximab) is highly suspicious for active tuberculosis (TB reactivation). Prior to starting anti-TNF therapy, latent TB screening (IGRA or TST) is required, and LTBI should be treated. Active TB requires holding anti-TNF therapy and completing full TB treatment (6-9 months) before re-evaluation. Anti-TNF therapy can significantly increase TB reactivation risk.

q: A 45-year-old woman with moderate persistent asthma (NAEPP Step 3) has symptoms most days of the week despite low-dose ICS. What is the preferred Step 3 regimen upgrade?
opts: A. Add oral prednisone 10mg daily
B. Add LABA to low-dose ICS
C. Double the ICS dose only
D. Switch to LTRA monotherapy
exp: NAEPP Step 3 preferred therapy is low-dose ICS + LABA (e.g., fluticasone/salmeterol). The combination reduces exacerbations, improves symptom control, and is superior to doubling ICS dose. LABAs should NEVER be used as monotherapy in asthma (increase asthma-related deaths). An LTRA (montelukast) is an alternative but less effective than ICS/LABA. Oral steroids are reserved for Step 6 or burst therapy during exacerbations.

q: A 68-year-old man with COPD GOLD Group E (mMRC dyspnea 3, 2 exacerbations last year) is on a LABA. What is the most appropriate intensification?
opts: A. Add ICS
B. Add LAMA
C. Switch to LAMA + LABA (dual bronchodilator)
D. Add roflumilast
exp: GOLD 2024 guidelines recommend LAMA + LABA (dual bronchodilator) for Group E patients who are symptomatic and have exacerbation history. Adding LAMA to LABA is the standard first intensification. ICS is added when blood eosinophils ≥300 cells/mcL or if patients remain uncontrolled on LAMA+LABA. Roflumilast (PDE4 inhibitor) is added for severe COPD with chronic bronchitis and frequent exacerbations on top of bronchodilators.

q: A 35-year-old man with major depressive disorder (MDD) is started on sertraline 50mg daily. After 4 weeks, he reports minimal improvement. What is the most appropriate next step?
opts: A. Discontinue sertraline and start bupropion
B. Increase sertraline to 100mg daily
C. Add alprazolam 0.5mg TID
D. Switch to an MAOI
exp: Adequate antidepressant trials require 4-8 weeks at therapeutic doses. After 4 weeks at 50mg sertraline (minimum effective dose), the appropriate step is to increase to 100mg (and can go to 200mg) before declaring treatment failure. Switching to a new agent is premature. Benzodiazepines are not antidepressants and should not be added for MDD. MAOIs have significant drug and food interactions and are not first-line.

q: A patient with bipolar I disorder, currently in a depressive episode, is asking about medication options. He has never tried any mood stabilizers. Which medication has the best evidence for bipolar depression?
opts: A. Sertraline monotherapy
B. Quetiapine monotherapy
C. Lithium monotherapy only
D. Lamotrigine monotherapy
exp: Quetiapine (Seroquel) has FDA approval and strong evidence for bipolar depression, both as monotherapy and adjunctive therapy. Lamotrigine has evidence for prevention of bipolar depression recurrence but is slower to titrate (5-6 weeks to therapeutic dose) and is less effective for acute episodes. Lithium is first-line for bipolar mania and maintenance but has limited data for acute bipolar depression. Antidepressant monotherapy (sertraline) without a mood stabilizer can trigger mania.

q: A 25-year-old woman with epilepsy and focal seizures is on levetiracetam 1000mg BID. She reports persistent irritability and mood changes. A second AED is being considered. Which agent is most appropriate?
opts: A. Valproic acid
B. Carbamazepine
C. Lamotrigine
D. Phenobarbital
exp: Lamotrigine is a good choice for focal seizures, has mood-stabilizing properties (helpful in this patient's case), and is generally well-tolerated. Valproic acid is effective but associated with weight gain, teratogenicity (neural tube defects), hair loss, and hepatotoxicity — less preferred in women of childbearing age. Carbamazepine is a potent CYP inducer with many drug interactions. Phenobarbital is sedating and has significant tolerance/dependence concerns.

q: A 72-year-old man with Parkinson disease has tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia. He is ready to start pharmacotherapy. Which is the most appropriate first-line agent for motor symptoms in a non-demented elderly patient?
opts: A. Trihexyphenidyl (anticholinergic)
B. Amantadine
C. Carbidopa/levodopa
D. Pramipexole (dopamine agonist)
exp: Carbidopa/levodopa is the most effective treatment for Parkinson motor symptoms and is first-line for older patients. Dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole) are preferred as initial therapy in younger patients (<65) to delay levodopa-associated motor fluctuations, but cause more hallucinations and impulse control disorders in elderly. Anticholinergics (trihexyphenidyl) worsen cognition in elderly — AVOID in older patients. Amantadine is used for dyskinesias.

q: A patient develops neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) after starting haloperidol. Which clinical features define NMS?
opts: A. Hyperthermia, diaphoresis, hypertension, and mydriasis
B. Hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, altered mental status, and autonomic instability
C. Hyperthermia, diarrhea, agitation, and tremor
D. Hypothermia, rigidity, coma, and bradycardia
exp: NMS tetrad: hyperthermia (often >40°C), lead-pipe muscle rigidity, altered mental status, and autonomic instability (tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis). Elevated CK (often >1000 IU/L) is a key lab finding. Treatment: discontinue offending agent (antipsychotic), supportive care, bromocriptine (dopamine agonist), dantrolene for severe hyperthermia/rigidity. Distinguish from serotonin syndrome (clonus, hyperreflexia, diarrhea, agitation — faster onset).

q: An 82-year-old woman is admitted with confusion. Her medication list includes diphenhydramine 25mg nightly, oxybutynin 5mg TID, and amitriptyline 25mg nightly. Which of these medications is most likely contributing to her confusion?
opts: A. All three are on the Beers Criteria and each contributes
B. Oxybutynin only
C. Amitriptyline only
D. Diphenhydramine only
exp: All three agents are strongly anticholinergic and listed on the AGS Beers Criteria as potentially inappropriate medications in older adults. The anticholinergic burden (ACB score) is additive and can cause delirium, falls, urinary retention, and constipation in elderly. Diphenhydramine (H1 blocker), oxybutynin (muscarinic antagonist), and amitriptyline (TCA with high anticholinergic activity) should all be avoided or discontinued. Substitute: melatonin for sleep, mirabegron for OAB, SNRI for pain/depression.

q: A 78-year-old man with osteoporosis and T-score of -2.8 at the hip is being considered for bisphosphonate therapy. He has no swallowing difficulties and can sit upright for 30+ minutes. He has CrCl of 28 mL/min. Which is the most appropriate agent?
opts: A. Oral alendronate 70mg weekly
B. IV zoledronic acid 5mg annually
C. Oral risedronate 35mg weekly
D. IV ibandronate 3mg every 3 months
exp: Oral bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate) are generally avoided when CrCl <30-35 mL/min due to risk of renal accumulation and AKI. IV zoledronic acid is the preferred agent for patients with severe CKD (manufacturer labeling states avoid if CrCl <35, but it is often used in CrCl 30-35 under careful monitoring; however, below 30 it is generally avoided). In patients below CrCl 30, alternatives include denosumab (RANKL inhibitor) which is renally safe. Ibandronate (IV) avoids CI with GI issues but still has renal concerns.

q: A patient undergoing chemotherapy with cisplatin and doxorubicin develops nausea and vomiting starting 1 hour after infusion and lasting 24 hours. Which antiemetic regimen is most appropriate?
opts: A. Ondansetron 8mg IV + dexamethasone 12mg
B. Ondansetron 8mg IV alone
C. Ondansetron 8mg IV + dexamethasone 12mg + NK1 antagonist (aprepitant 125mg)
D. Prochlorperazine 10mg IV only
exp: Cisplatin is highly emetogenic (HEC). ASCO/NCCN guidelines for HEC: triple therapy — 5-HT3 antagonist (ondansetron) + dexamethasone + NK1 receptor antagonist (aprepitant or fosaprepitant). Doxorubicin alone is moderately emetogenic but the combination makes this HEC. Ondansetron alone is insufficient for HEC. Adding olanzapine (4th agent) is recommended for highly refractory nausea.

q: A patient with cancer develops tumor lysis syndrome (TLS) 24 hours after starting chemotherapy. Labs show K+ 6.8, phosphate 8.2, Ca 6.9, uric acid 12.4, creatinine 3.1. Which agent specifically targets uric acid?
opts: A. Allopurinol
B. Rasburicase
C. Sodium bicarbonate IV
D. Kayexalate
exp: Rasburicase (recombinant urate oxidase) rapidly converts existing uric acid to allantoin (which is 5-10× more soluble). It is used for established TLS or high-risk patients. Allopurinol inhibits new uric acid production but does NOT reduce existing uric acid levels — it is used for TLS prophylaxis. Rasburicase is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency (causes hemolysis). Sodium bicarbonate was historically used but is no longer routinely recommended.

q: A new anticoagulant is tested in an RCT. The stroke rate in the treatment group is 1.5% vs 3.0% in the placebo group. What is the Number Needed to Treat (NNT)?
exp: ARR (Absolute Risk Reduction) = 3.0% - 1.5% = 1.5% = 0.015. NNT = 1/ARR = 1/0.015 = 66.7 ≈ 67. The NNT means 67 patients must be treated to prevent 1 additional stroke compared to placebo. Relative Risk Reduction (RRR) = ARR/control rate = 1.5/3.0 = 50% — but RRR overstates the benefit and NNT/ARR is more clinically meaningful.

q: A study reports a hazard ratio (HR) of 0.72 (95% CI: 0.58-0.89) for cardiovascular death with a new drug vs placebo. How should this be interpreted?
opts: A. The drug increases CV death risk by 28%
B. The drug reduces CV death risk by 28%; statistically significant
C. The drug reduces CV death risk by 28%; not statistically significant
D. The drug has no effect; CI crosses 1.0
exp: HR of 0.72 = 28% reduction in CV death risk (1 - 0.72 = 0.28 = 28%). The 95% CI (0.58-0.89) does NOT include 1.0, so the result IS statistically significant (p <0.05). If the CI included 1.0 (e.g., 0.58-1.12), the result would not be statistically significant. HR is a ratio — values <1 favor treatment, values >1 favor control.

q: A pharmacist reviews a case-control study examining the association between proton pump inhibitors and C. difficile infection. The odds ratio is 1.8 (95% CI 1.2-2.7). What does this mean?
opts: A. PPI users have 80% higher odds of CDI; this is a relative risk
B. PPI users have 1.8 times the odds of CDI; the association is statistically significant
C. PPI use reduces CDI risk by 80%
D. The study is invalid because OR >1.0
exp: OR of 1.8 means PPI users have 1.8 times the odds (80% higher odds) of developing CDI compared to non-users. The 95% CI (1.2-2.7) does not include 1.0, confirming statistical significance. Odds ratio is used in case-control studies (not RCTs). It approximates relative risk when the outcome is rare (<10%). This is an association, not causation — confounders must be considered.

q: A meta-analysis funnel plot shows significant asymmetry, with studies skewed toward positive results. What type of bias does this suggest?
opts: A. Confounding bias
B. Selection bias
C. Publication bias
D. Measurement bias
exp: Funnel plot asymmetry in meta-analyses is a sign of publication bias — studies with positive results are more likely to be published than negative/null studies, skewing the overall estimate of effect. This inflates meta-analytic conclusions. The Egger test or Begg test can statistically assess funnel plot asymmetry. Confounding and selection bias affect individual study validity rather than the aggregate literature.

q: A prospective cohort study follows 10,000 statin users and 10,000 non-users for 10 years. The incidence of MI is 3% in statin users and 5% in non-users. What is the relative risk?
opts: C. 2.0 (non-users vs users)
exp: Relative Risk (RR) = Risk in exposed / Risk in unexposed = 3% / 5% = 0.60. An RR of 0.60 means statin users have 60% the risk of MI compared to non-users (40% risk reduction). RR = 1.0 means no association. RR < 1 means protective effect. Note: Cohort studies use relative risk; case-control studies use odds ratio.

q: A randomized controlled trial has an intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis. A patient in the treatment arm discontinues therapy at week 4 but continues to be followed and outcomes are recorded. How is this patient's data handled in ITT analysis?
opts: A. Excluded from the analysis
B. Analyzed in the group they were originally randomized to
C. Crossed over to the control group
D. Counted as a treatment failure
exp: ITT analysis includes all randomized patients in their originally assigned groups regardless of protocol adherence, dropout, or crossover. This preserves the benefit of randomization and provides a conservative estimate of treatment effect. Per-protocol (PP) analysis only includes patients who completed the protocol — results in potential overestimation of efficacy. ITT is the standard for superiority trials; PP may be used as sensitivity analysis.

q: A pharmacist identifies that a patient is prescribed warfarin and is self-medicating with St. John's Wort for depression. What is the clinical significance of this interaction?
opts: A. St. John's Wort inhibits warfarin metabolism, increasing bleeding risk
B. St. John's Wort induces CYP2C9 and P-gp, reducing warfarin effect and increasing clotting risk
C. No significant interaction
D. St. John's Wort inhibits P-gp only, increasing warfarin absorption
exp: St. John's Wort is a potent inducer of CYP3A4, CYP2C9, and P-glycoprotein. It significantly reduces warfarin plasma levels (by ~30-70%), leading to subtherapeutic INR and increased risk of thromboembolism. This is a major drug-herb interaction. Patients should be counseled to avoid concurrent use. Other herbal inducers: echinacea, ginseng. Herbal inhibitors increasing warfarin effect: garlic, ginkgo, ginger, fish oil.

q: A medical information request asks about the dose of intravenous immune globulin (IVIG) for immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP). Which resource is most appropriate for primary drug information?
opts: A. Lexi-Comp or Micromedex (tertiary database)
B. Manufacturer package insert
C. Primary literature (original RCTs/guidelines)
D. Pharmacist colleague
exp: For a specific clinical query like ITP dosing, primary literature (RCTs, systematic reviews, clinical guidelines from ASH) provides the highest level of evidence. Tertiary resources (Lexi-Comp, Micromedex) are compiled summaries — useful for quick reference but may lag behind current evidence. Package inserts provide FDA-approved dosing but off-label uses are not included. This aligns with BCPS Domain 3: using highest-quality evidence for practice decisions.

q: A pharmacist wants to evaluate a newly approved drug using the highest level of evidence for safety in a large population. Which study design is most appropriate post-market?
opts: A. Case report
B. Retrospective case-control study
C. Large prospective post-marketing cohort study
D. Expert opinion panel
exp: Post-marketing surveillance using large prospective cohort studies (or registry studies) provides the best real-world safety data for newly approved drugs. Phase IV post-marketing studies can detect rare adverse events not identified in pre-approval trials (which typically have insufficient sample sizes for rare events). Active surveillance programs (FDA Sentinel System, FAERS) are important tools. Case reports identify potential signals but cannot establish causality.

q: A 55-year-old male smoker has LDL 190 mg/dL, no ASCVD, 10-year risk 8%. On high-intensity statin, LDL is still 145 mg/dL. What is the next best step?
opts: A. Add niacin
B. Add ezetimibe 10mg daily
C. Switch to low-intensity statin
D. No change needed
exp: When LDL goal is not met on maximally-tolerated statin, ezetimibe is the first add-on agent (ACC/AHA). For very high-risk patients with LDL >70 on max statin plus ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors are next. Niacin has not shown CV outcome benefit. This patient has possible familial hypercholesterolemia given LDL 190 without ASCVD.

q: A 48-year-old woman with HTN on HCTZ 25mg has K+ 3.0 mEq/L and glucose 118 mg/dL. Most appropriate management?
opts: A. Stop HCTZ, switch to amlodipine
B. Add KCl supplementation and continue HCTZ
C. Add spironolactone 25mg and reduce HCTZ dose
D. Switch to furosemide
exp: Thiazides cause hypokalemia and hyperglycemia. Adding spironolactone corrects hypokalemia, adds BP lowering, and allows HCTZ dose reduction. Simple KCl supplementation is less effective for chronic thiazide-induced hypokalemia. Furosemide causes even more electrolyte losses.

q: A patient with HFpEF (EF 55%) presents with fluid overload. Which therapy has shown the strongest outcome benefit specifically in HFpEF?
opts: A. Sacubitril/valsartan
B. Carvedilol
C. Spironolactone
D. SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin or dapagliflozin)
exp: EMPEROR-Preserved (empagliflozin) and DELIVER (dapagliflozin) trials demonstrated SGLT2 inhibitors reduce HF hospitalization in HFpEF. Empagliflozin is FDA-approved for HFpEF. Sacubitril/valsartan showed neutral mortality in PARAGON-HF. SGLT2i are now first-line agents for HFpEF pharmacotherapy.

q: A patient with first provoked DVT (caused by prolonged immobility) completed 3 months of anticoagulation. What is the recommended duration at this point?
opts: A. 3 months total is sufficient -- stop now
B. Continue 3 more months (6 total)
C. Indefinite anticoagulation
D. 12 months total
exp: For first provoked VTE with transient reversible risk factor, 3 months of anticoagulation is sufficient. Recurrence risk is low once the provoking factor resolves. Extended anticoagulation is reserved for unprovoked VTE, recurrent VTE, or high-risk thrombophilias (antiphospholipid syndrome, Factor V Leiden homozygous, protein C/S deficiency).

q: A patient on amiodarone develops progressive dyspnea, dry cough, and CT shows diffuse ground-glass opacities. DLCO is decreased. Most likely cause?
opts: A. New congestive heart failure
B. Amiodarone-induced pulmonary toxicity
C. Pneumococcal pneumonia
D. Pulmonary embolism
exp: Amiodarone pulmonary toxicity (1-7% incidence) presents with progressive dyspnea, dry cough, and bilateral ground-glass opacities on CT. DLCO is the most sensitive PFT finding. Treatment: discontinue amiodarone and start corticosteroids. Other amiodarone toxicities: thyroid dysfunction, hepatotoxicity, corneal microdeposits, blue-gray skin discoloration, peripheral neuropathy.

q: After DES stent placement for STEMI, how long should dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) be continued?
opts: A. 1 month
B. 3 months
C. At least 12 months
D. Lifelong
exp: DAPT (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor) should be continued for at least 12 months after DES placement for ACS to prevent stent thrombosis. High-bleeding-risk patients may have DAPT shortened to 3-6 months with newer generation DES. After 12 months, continue aspirin indefinitely. Ticagrelor may be extended to 3 years (PEGASUS-TIMI 54) for high ischemic risk.

q: A 30-year-old pregnant woman (32 weeks) has asymptomatic bacteriuria with >100,000 CFU/mL E. coli. Management?
opts: A. No treatment required
B. Nitrofurantoin 100mg ER BID x 5 days
C. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BID x 7 days
D. TMP-SMX DS BID x 3 days
exp: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy requires treatment due to high risk of progression to pyelonephritis (25-30%) and preterm birth. Nitrofurantoin is first-line in first and second trimester. Avoid in third trimester (hemolytic anemia risk). Avoid TMP-SMX in first trimester (folate antagonist) and near term (kernicterus). Fluoroquinolones are generally avoided in pregnancy.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: An HIV-positive patient has CD4 count of 180. Which prophylactic regimen should be initiated?
opts: A. Fluconazole for Candida prophylaxis
B. TMP-SMX DS daily for PCP prophylaxis
C. Azithromycin 1200mg weekly for MAC
D. Acyclovir for HSV prophylaxis
exp: PCP (Pneumocystis jirovecii) prophylaxis with TMP-SMX DS is indicated when CD4 <200. It also covers toxoplasmosis and bacterial infections. MAC prophylaxis (azithromycin) is indicated when CD4 <50. Toxoplasmosis prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) when CD4 <100 with positive Toxoplasma IgG. TMP-SMX is the cornerstone of OI prophylaxis.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: An HIV patient (CD4 45) has cavitary lung lesion with positive India ink on BAL. Preferred induction therapy?
opts: A. Fluconazole 800mg daily
B. Amphotericin B deoxycholate + flucytosine x 2 weeks
C. Voriconazole 6mg/kg q12h
D. Itraconazole 200mg BID
exp: Cryptococcal meningitis/pulmonary disease induction: amphotericin B plus flucytosine (5-FC) 100 mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses for 2 weeks, then consolidation with fluconazole 400mg daily for 8 weeks, then fluconazole 200mg maintenance. Liposomal amphotericin B preferred when available due to less nephrotoxicity. Fluconazole monotherapy is inferior for induction.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: A patient with penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis) needs treatment for primary syphilis. Best alternative?
opts: A. Ceftriaxone 1g IM x 10 days
B. Azithromycin 2g single dose
C. Doxycycline 100mg BID x 14 days
D. Penicillin G desensitization required for all stages
exp: For primary/secondary syphilis in penicillin-allergic non-pregnant patients, doxycycline 100mg BID x 14 days is the CDC-recommended alternative. Azithromycin resistance is increasing and is NOT recommended. For neurosyphilis or pregnancy, penicillin G is the ONLY acceptable treatment - desensitization is required in allergic pregnant patients.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: A drug has half-life of 6 hours. A 1000mg IV dose is given. How much remains after 18 hours?
opts: A. 500mg
B. 250mg
C. 125mg
exp: 18 hours / 6 hours per half-life = 3 half-lives elapsed. Starting 1000mg: after t1 = 500mg, after t2 = 250mg, after t3 = 125mg. First-order kinetics: constant fraction eliminated per unit time. Steady state reached in 4-5 half-lives (24-30 hours).

q: Rifampin is added to phenytoin therapy. What happens to phenytoin levels?
opts: A. Increase - rifampin inhibits CYP2C9
B. Decrease - rifampin induces CYP2C9 and CYP3A4
C. No change
D. Increase - rifampin increases GI absorption
exp: Rifampin is a potent CYP enzyme inducer (CYP2C9, CYP3A4, P-gp). Phenytoin is a CYP2C9 substrate. Rifampin significantly increases phenytoin metabolism, decreasing levels and risking breakthrough seizures. Monitor phenytoin levels closely. Interaction maximal within 5-7 days. Rifampin also reduces levels of warfarin, DOACs, oral contraceptives, statins, and many others.

q: A patient on empagliflozin reports recurrent vaginal yeast infections. What counseling is appropriate?
opts: A. Stop empagliflozin
B. This is an expected adverse effect; maintain good genital hygiene
C. Take prophylactic fluconazole weekly
D. Switch to an SGLT1 inhibitor
exp: SGLT2 inhibitors increase urinary glucose (glucosuria), creating a favorable environment for genital Candida infections. This is an expected class effect. Patients should maintain good hygiene and report symptoms. Antifungal treatment when infections occur. Other important SGLT2i adverse effects to counsel on: euglycemic DKA, volume depletion, UTI, and Fournier gangrene (rare but serious necrotizing fasciitis of the perineum).

q: A patient with type 2 DM starts GLP-1 agonist therapy (semaglutide). Which adverse effect most commonly causes discontinuation?
opts: A. Hypoglycemia
B. Nausea and vomiting
C. Pancreatitis
D. Thyroid C-cell tumors
exp: GLP-1 receptor agonists commonly cause nausea and vomiting (20-50% incidence), which is the most common reason for discontinuation. These GI effects are dose-dependent and typically subside after several weeks. Starting at lowest dose and titrating slowly minimizes GI side effects. GLP-1 RAs carry a warning for pancreatitis (rare) and thyroid C-cell tumors (animal data; contraindicated in personal/family history of MTC or MEN2). They do not cause hypoglycemia as monotherapy.

q: A patient with adrenal insufficiency on hydrocortisone needs stress dosing for moderate surgery (knee replacement). Appropriate regimen?
opts: A. No change; continue home doses
B. Hydrocortisone 50mg IV day of surgery then taper over 1-2 days
C. Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h x 3 days
D. Dexamethasone 4mg once preoperatively
exp: For moderate surgical stress, hydrocortisone 25-50mg IV on day of surgery then taper to home dose over 1-2 days is appropriate. This replaces the cortisol stress response patients with adrenal insufficiency cannot generate. For minor procedures: double home dose. For major surgery/septic shock: hydrocortisone 200-300mg/day in divided doses.

q: A patient with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 22) has serum bicarbonate of 18 mEq/L. Which intervention slows CKD progression in this setting?
opts: A. Sodium bicarbonate supplementation to maintain HCO3 > 22
B. Increase dietary protein intake
C. Start dialysis
D. IV acetazolamide
exp: KDIGO recommends sodium bicarbonate supplementation to maintain serum bicarbonate at or above 22 mEq/L in CKD. Chronic metabolic acidosis accelerates GFR decline, muscle catabolism, and bone disease. Oral NaHCO3 tablets (650mg 2-3x/day) are first-line. Sodium citrate is an alternative. Protein restriction (0.6-0.8 g/kg/day) also slows progression.

q: A patient with SIADH has serum sodium 122 mEq/L with confusion. Which treatment is most appropriate?
opts: A. 3% NaCl at 1-2 mL/kg/h
B. Free water restriction alone
C. Normal saline at 125 mL/h
D. Furosemide 40mg IV
exp: Symptomatic hyponatremia (sodium <125 with neurologic symptoms) requires correction with 3% hypertonic saline at 1-2 mL/kg/h, targeting 1-2 mEq/L/hour correction until symptoms resolve. Max 8-10 mEq/L increase in 24 hours to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine myelinolysis). Normal saline is ineffective in SIADH (urine may be more concentrated). Free water restriction alone is for asymptomatic chronic SIADH.

q: A malnourished alcoholic starts enteral nutrition. On day 2, phosphate drops to 0.8 mg/dL and he develops respiratory failure and weakness. What syndrome is this?
opts: A. Hypomagnesemia
B. Refeeding syndrome
C. Pseudohyponatremia
D. Hyperphosphatemia
exp: Refeeding syndrome occurs when malnourished patients receive carbohydrates (insulin release drives phosphate, potassium, magnesium intracellularly). Severe hypophosphatemia (<1.0 mg/dL) causes respiratory failure, cardiac arrhythmias, hemolytic anemia, and neuromuscular weakness. Prevention: correct electrolytes before refeeding, start at 10-20 kcal/kg/day and advance slowly. High risk: alcoholics, anorexia, prolonged NPO, cancer cachexia.

q: A patient with generalized tonic-clonic seizures refractory to levetiracetam is started on valproic acid. She is of childbearing age. What counseling is essential?
opts: A. Take with food to avoid nausea
B. Valproate is associated with neural tube defects and reduced cognitive outcomes in offspring
C. Must use only valproate brand name (Depakote)
D. Monthly CBC required
exp: Valproic acid is a major teratogen — associated with neural tube defects (2-3%), cardiac malformations, and lower IQ in exposed children (NEAD study). Women of childbearing potential must use effective contraception. Valproate should be avoided in pregnancy when possible; alternatives include lamotrigine or levetiracetam. Folic acid 4-5mg/day pre-conception is recommended if valproate cannot be avoided. This is an FDA black box warning.

q: A 68-year-old man with Alzheimer disease has moderate symptoms (MMSE 14/30). Which pharmacotherapy has evidence for symptom improvement?
opts: A. Memantine 10mg BID alone
B. Donepezil 10mg daily alone
C. Donepezil + memantine combination
D. Rivastigmine patch only
exp: For moderate-severe Alzheimer disease, combination therapy with a cholinesterase inhibitor (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) plus memantine (NMDA antagonist) is supported by clinical evidence and guidelines. Memantine modulates glutamate excitotoxicity. Cholinesterase inhibitors prevent ACh breakdown. The combination is more effective than either alone for moderate-severe disease. None modify the disease course; they provide modest symptomatic benefit.

q: A patient has a first episode of major depressive disorder. After successful treatment with an antidepressant, how long should therapy be continued after full remission?
opts: A. 1 month, then discontinue
B. 4-9 months after remission (total course 6-12 months)
C. Indefinitely (lifetime)
D. 3 months, then dose reduction
exp: After achieving remission from a first MDD episode, antidepressant therapy should be continued for 4-9 months beyond remission (total treatment 6-12 months) to prevent relapse. Abrupt discontinuation causes discontinuation syndrome. Indefinite therapy is recommended after 3 or more episodes, or 2 episodes with risk factors (severe symptoms, early onset, strong family history). Gradual tapering over 2-4 weeks when discontinuing.

q: An ICU patient develops torsades de pointes (TdP) with QTc 540ms. He is on haloperidol and azithromycin. K+ is 3.1, Mg2+ is 1.2. What is the first-line treatment?
opts: A. Amiodarone 150mg IV
B. Magnesium sulfate 2g IV
C. Synchronized cardioversion
D. Lidocaine 1.5mg/kg IV
exp: Torsades de pointes (polymorphic VT with prolonged QT) is treated with IV magnesium sulfate 2g IV even if magnesium levels are normal. Additionally, correct hypokalemia (target K+ >4.0), discontinue offending drugs (haloperidol, azithromycin), increase heart rate with isoproterenol or overdrive pacing (shortens QT). Amiodarone prolongs QT and is contraindicated. Synchronized cardioversion for hemodynamic instability.

q: A 22-year-old woman requests emergency contraception after unprotected sex 36 hours ago. She has no contraindications. Which is the most effective option?
opts: A. Plan B (levonorgestrel) 1.5mg
B. Ella (ulipristal acetate) 30mg
C. Combined oral contraceptive (Yuzpe method)
D. Copper IUD insertion
exp: Copper IUD insertion (within 5 days) is the most effective emergency contraception (>99% effective) and also provides ongoing contraception. Ulipristal acetate (ella) is more effective than levonorgestrel EC, especially 72-120 hours after intercourse. Levonorgestrel (Plan B) is most effective within 72 hours but effectiveness decreases over time. The Yuzpe method (combined OCP) is the least effective option. For this patient at 36 hours, copper IUD > ulipristal > levonorgestrel.

q: A patient with HIV is starting antiretroviral therapy. Her CD4 is 380 and HIV RNA is 45,000 copies/mL. She has no resistance mutations. Which regimen is preferred first-line?
opts: A. Zidovudine + lamivudine + efavirenz
B. Bictegravir/tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine (BIC/TAF/FTC, Biktarvy)
C. Lopinavir/ritonavir + two NRTIs
D. Abacavir/lamivudine + dolutegravir (if HLA-B*5701 negative)
exp: DHHS guidelines recommend integrase strand transfer inhibitor (INSTI)-based regimens as preferred first-line therapy. Biktarvy (BIC/TAF/FTC) is a single-tablet regimen with high efficacy, minimal drug interactions, and high barrier to resistance. Dolutegravir-based regimens (Triumeq or Dovato) are also preferred. Efavirenz (NNRTI) is no longer preferred due to CNS side effects. Boosted PI regimens (lopinavir/ritonavir) have higher pill burden and metabolic effects.
ref: Vol 1, Ch 4-5: Infectious Diseases I & II

q: A 2-year-old child (weight 12kg) is diagnosed with acute otitis media (AOM). He has no penicillin allergy and has not received antibiotics in the past 30 days. Which treatment is preferred?
opts: A. Amoxicillin 40mg/kg/day divided BID
B. Amoxicillin 80-90mg/kg/day divided BID
C. Azithromycin 10mg/kg on day 1 then 5mg/kg x 4 days
D. No antibiotics; watchful waiting only
exp: For AOM in children 2 years old (especially bilateral or with otorrhea), antibiotic treatment is recommended. High-dose amoxicillin 80-90 mg/kg/day divided BID is first-line (to overcome intermediate-resistant S. pneumoniae). Standard dose (40 mg/kg) may be insufficient. Azithromycin has poor S. pneumoniae activity and is only for penicillin-allergic patients (type I hypersensitivity). Watchful waiting is appropriate for non-severe AOM in children ≥2 years.

q: A 6-month-old (weight 7kg) develops RSV bronchiolitis with SpO2 90% on room air. Which treatment is indicated?
opts: A. Albuterol nebulization q4h
B. Nebulized epinephrine
C. Supplemental oxygen
D. Systemic corticosteroids
exp: Bronchiolitis management is primarily supportive. Supplemental oxygen is the most important intervention when SpO2 <90-92%. AAP guidelines: bronchodilators (albuterol, epinephrine) are NOT routinely recommended (no consistent benefit). Corticosteroids are NOT recommended. High-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) or CPAP for severe cases. Ribavirin is not routinely used. Palivizumab (RSV prophylaxis) for high-risk infants (premature <29 weeks, hemodynamically significant CHD, CLD).

q: A cancer patient on chemotherapy develops febrile neutropenia (ANC <500, temp 38.5 C). Which empiric antibiotic is most appropriate?
opts: A. Oral ciprofloxacin + amoxicillin-clavulanate (low-risk)
B. IV piperacillin-tazobactam (high-risk inpatient)
C. Oral doxycycline
D. IV vancomycin alone
exp: High-risk febrile neutropenia (ANC <100, prolonged neutropenia >7 days, inpatient) requires IV broad-spectrum anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam: cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam, or a carbapenem. Vancomycin alone has no Gram-negative coverage. Low-risk patients (Multinational Association for Supportive Care in Cancer MASCC score >21) can be managed with oral ciprofloxacin + amoxicillin-clavulanate. G-CSF (filgrastim/pegfilgrastim) is used as prophylaxis, not treatment of established neutropenia.

q: A patient on chemotherapy develops bone marrow suppression with ANC nadir of 200 cells/mcL. G-CSF (filgrastim) was not given prophylactically. Which guideline threshold determines when primary G-CSF prophylaxis should be used?
opts: A. When febrile neutropenia risk is >10%
B. When febrile neutropenia risk is >20%
C. When ANC falls below 500 on the first cycle
D. Only for patients over age 65
exp: Primary G-CSF prophylaxis is recommended when the chemotherapy regimen carries >20% risk of febrile neutropenia (ASCO/NCCN guidelines). For intermediate risk (10-20%), individual patient risk factors (age >65, poor performance status, extensive prior therapy, open wounds) should guide the decision. G-CSF reduces febrile neutropenia incidence, hospitalization, and infection-related mortality. Pegfilgrastim (long-acting) is preferred over filgrastim for convenience.

q: A pharmacist participates in a quality improvement project using a Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) cycle to reduce medication errors. Which step involves analyzing the results of the intervention?
opts: A. Plan
B. Do
C. Study
D. Act
exp: The PDSA cycle: Plan (identify problem, design intervention), Do (implement the change on a small scale), Study (analyze results, compare to predictions, identify what was learned), Act (adopt, adapt, or abandon based on findings then start new cycle). This is the cornerstone of continuous quality improvement in healthcare. Other QI frameworks include Six Sigma (DMAIC) and Lean methodologies.

q: A hospital wants to measure the percentage of patients with community-acquired pneumonia who received appropriate antibiotics within 4 hours of arrival. This is an example of which type of quality measure?
opts: A. Outcome measure
B. Process measure
C. Balancing measure
D. Structural measure
exp: A process measure assesses whether a specific step in care delivery was performed (e.g., antibiotic timing, pneumococcal vaccine given). Outcome measures assess the result of care (e.g., mortality, readmission rates). Structural measures assess the system capacity (e.g., pharmacist-to-patient ratios, EHR use). Balancing measures ensure that improving one aspect does not negatively impact another. Process measures are easiest to collect but may not always correlate with improved outcomes.

q: A 72-year-old with COPD receives influenza vaccination. Which additional vaccine is now also recommended at this visit?
opts: A. Pneumococcal vaccine (PCV20 or PCV15+PPSV23)
B. Shingrix (RZV) dose 1
C. Hepatitis B vaccine
D. All of the above are appropriate at this visit
exp: Adults aged 65 and older should receive: (1) Annual influenza vaccine, (2) Pneumococcal vaccine (PCV20 alone OR PCV15 followed by PPSV23 in 1 year) if not previously vaccinated, and (3) Shingrix (recombinant zoster vaccine) 2-dose series starting at age 50. Hepatitis B vaccine (Heplisav-B 2-dose or Engerix-B 3-dose) is now recommended for all adults through age 59, and shared decision-making for 60+. Multiple vaccines can be administered at the same visit.

q: A 78-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation is on warfarin. Her CHADS2-VASc is 5. She asks about bleeding risk. Which tool quantifies bleeding risk in AFib patients on anticoagulation?
opts: A. CURB-65
B. GRACE score
C. HAS-BLED score
D. TIMI risk score
exp: The HAS-BLED score quantifies bleeding risk in AFib patients on anticoagulation: Hypertension (uncontrolled), Abnormal renal/liver function, Stroke history, Bleeding history/predisposition, Labile INR, Elderly (>65), Drugs/alcohol use. A high HAS-BLED score should prompt risk factor modification (not necessarily withhold anticoagulation). CURB-65 is for CAP severity. GRACE score is for ACS. TIMI score is for ACS risk stratification.

q: A patient with COPD exacerbation is hospitalized. He requires systemic corticosteroids. What is the recommended duration?
opts: A. 3 days
B. 5 days
C. 14 days
D. 30 days
exp: GOLD guidelines recommend a 5-day course of systemic corticosteroids for COPD exacerbations (prednisone 40mg daily x 5 days). The REDUCE trial demonstrated that 5 days is non-inferior to 14 days with fewer adverse effects. Longer courses increase risk of hyperglycemia, infections, and adrenal suppression. Oral prednisone is equivalent to IV methylprednisolone in patients who can swallow.

q: An 82-year-old woman on multiple medications falls. A comprehensive medication review is performed. Which medication class is most strongly associated with fall risk in the elderly?
opts: A. ACE inhibitors
B. Beta-blockers
C. Benzodiazepines and sedative-hypnotics
D. Proton pump inhibitors
exp: Benzodiazepines, sedative-hypnotics (zolpidem, zaleplon), and other CNS depressants are most strongly associated with fall and fracture risk in elderly patients (Beers Criteria). Other high-risk fall medications: opioids, antipsychotics, anticholinergics, antiepileptics, antidepressants (TCAs > SSRIs). A medication-related fall risk assessment should include reviewing the medication list for these drug classes and deprescribing where possible.

q: A patient with moderate-severe plaque psoriasis is started on methotrexate 15mg weekly. Which monitoring is required?
opts: A. Monthly CBC and LFTs; annual liver biopsy after cumulative dose 1.5g
B. Baseline and periodic CBC, LFTs, renal function; liver biopsy if abnormal LFTs
C. CBC only; no LFT monitoring needed
D. Chest X-ray annually for pulmonary toxicity
exp: Methotrexate monitoring: baseline CBC with differential, LFTs, creatinine, hepatitis B/C screening. During therapy: CBC and LFTs every 4-8 weeks (monthly at initiation). Routine liver biopsy is no longer recommended; instead, use FIB-4 or liver elastography to assess fibrosis risk. Pulmonary toxicity (pneumonitis) can occur -- monitor for new respiratory symptoms. Teratogenic -- requires effective contraception.

q: A 55-year-old man has hypertension and is started on lisinopril. Two weeks later he calls complaining of a persistent dry cough. What is the most appropriate next step?
opts: A. Add benzonatate for cough suppression
B. Reduce lisinopril dose
C. Switch to an ARB (e.g., losartan)
D. Order chest X-ray to rule out pneumonia
exp: ACE inhibitor-induced cough occurs in 10-15% of patients (higher in Asian populations) due to bradykinin accumulation. It is a class effect and occurs regardless of dose. The appropriate action is to switch to an ARB (e.g., losartan, valsartan), which provides equivalent BP and renoprotective benefits without cough. ARBs block angiotensin II at the receptor but do not affect bradykinin degradation. Reducing dose does not resolve the cough.

q: 55yo M on warfarin (INR 2.4) started fluconazole for Candida UTI. Day 3 INR 6.8. Mechanism?
opts: A. Albumin displacement
B. CYP2C9 inhibition
C. CYP2C9 induction
D. Vitamin K malabsorption
exp: Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9, reducing S-warfarin metabolism. INR increases significantly. Dose reduction and monitoring required.
opts_exp: A. Minor; rarely clinically significant.
B. CORRECT - CYP2C9 inhibition is primary mechanism.
C. Would cause opposite effect (decrease INR).
D. Not primary mechanism; fluconazole doesn't affect vitamin K absorption.

q: Patient on simvastatin 40mg prescribed clarithromycin. Most appropriate action?
opts: A. Continue simvastatin at same dose; safe
B. Hold simvastatin during clarithromycin therapy
C. Reduce simvastatin to 10mg during clarithromycin
D. Switch antibiotic to azithromycin
exp: Clarithromycin is strong CYP3A4 inhibitor. Simvastatin AUC can increase 10-fold, causing myopathy/rhabdomyolysis. Hold simvastatin during short antibiotic course.
opts_exp: A. Dangerous - interaction magnitude too large.
B. CORRECT - Safest approach for short-course antibiotics.
C. Insufficient mitigation; interaction too large.
D. Azithromycin has less CYP3A4 inhibition but clarithromycin often preferred clinically.

q: Patient on clopidogrel post-PCI prescribed omeprazole for GI. Problem?
opts: A. Omeprazole induces CYP2C19, increasing activation
B. Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19, reducing clopidogrel activation
C. Omeprazole increases clopidogrel absorption unpredictably
D. Omeprazole causes direct platelet activation opposing effect
exp: Clopidogrel is CYP2C19 prodrug. Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19, reducing activation ~45%. Reduces antiplatelet efficacy. Pantoprazole preferred when PPI necessary.
opts_exp: A. Wrong direction - omeprazole is inhibitor.
B. CORRECT - CYP2C19 inhibition reduces active metabolite formation.
C. Omeprazole doesn't significantly affect absorption.
D. Omeprazole has no direct platelet effects.

q: Patient on tacrolimus post-kidney transplant started on voriconazole for fungal. Tacrolimus levels 4× above goal day 3. Mechanism?
opts: A. Voriconazole induces P-glycoprotein, increasing efflux
B. Voriconazole inhibits CYP3A4 and CYP2C19, reducing metabolism
C. Voriconazole alkalinizes urine, reducing renal clearance
D. Voriconazole directly binds tacrolimus at FKBP-12
exp: Tacrolimus is CYP3A4 substrate with narrow TI. Voriconazole potently inhibits CYP3A4. Requires empiric 50-75% dose reduction with close monitoring.
opts_exp: A. Voriconazole inhibits, not induces P-gp.
B. CORRECT - Potent CYP3A4 inhibition dramatically reduces clearance.
C. Tacrolimus is hepatically metabolized, not renally.
D. Voriconazole has no direct FKBP-12 binding interaction.

q: Patient on methadone maintenance started on rifampin for TB. Most likely consequence?
opts: A. Increased methadone levels, respiratory depression risk
B. Decreased methadone levels, likely opioid withdrawal
C. Prolonged methadone half-life, QTc elongation
D. No significant interaction between drugs
exp: Rifampin is potent CYP3A4/2B6 inducer. Methadone metabolized by CYP3A4/2B6. Induction increases clearance 60-80%. Significant dose increases needed to prevent withdrawal.
opts_exp: A. Inducers decrease, not increase drug levels.
B. CORRECT - Increased clearance 60-80% causes withdrawal.
C. Rifampin shortens, not prolongs half-life.
D. This is a well-documented significant pharmacokinetic interaction.

q: Patient on lithium for bipolar develops AKI, given high-dose ibuprofen. Lithium level 2.8 (prev 0.9). Primary mechanism?
opts: A. Ibuprofen directly inhibits lithium renal tubular secretion
B. NSAIDs reduce prostaglandin synthesis, decreasing renal perfusion and lithium clearance
C. Ibuprofen increases lithium protein binding, reducing distribution
D. NSAIDs alkalinize urine, reducing lithium ionization and excretion
exp: Lithium entirely renally eliminated. NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandins maintaining renal perfusion. Reduces GFR and increases proximal tubular sodium (and lithium) reabsorption. Decreased lithium clearance causes toxicity.
opts_exp: A. Lithium filtered freely, reabsorbed like sodium; not actively secreted.
B. CORRECT - Prostaglandin inhibition reduces GFR and increases reabsorption.
C. Lithium is free ion, not protein-bound.
D. Reabsorption follows sodium; not ionization-trapping mechanism.

q: Patient on carbamazepine for epilepsy started on oral contraceptives. Appropriate counseling?
opts: A. Carbamazepine has no effect on contraceptive hormones
B. Carbamazepine inhibits CYP3A4, increasing hormone levels and efficacy
C. Carbamazepine induces CYP3A4, reducing hormone levels and efficacy
D. Carbamazepine competes with estrogen for receptor binding
exp: Carbamazepine is potent CYP3A4 inducer. Oral contraceptives metabolized by CYP3A4. Induction accelerates hormone metabolism, significantly reducing levels. Contraceptive failure likely. Non-hormonal contraception recommended.
opts_exp: A. Significant interaction exists.
B. Carbamazepine induces, not inhibits CYP3A4.
C. CORRECT - Strong CYP3A4 induction reduces hormone efficacy.
D. No receptor-level competition.

q: 68yo M on digoxin 0.125mg daily started on amiodarone for AFib. Most critical monitoring?
opts: A. Monitor LFTs weekly for hepatotoxicity
B. Monitor digoxin levels and signs of toxicity due to P-glycoprotein inhibition
C. Monitor BNP for worsening heart failure
D. Monitor QTc for excessive prolongation from additive effects
exp: Amiodarone inhibits P-glycoprotein and CYP3A4/2D6, reducing digoxin renal and non-renal clearance. Digoxin levels increase 50-100%. Empiric 50% dose reduction and close monitoring essential.
opts_exp: A. LFT monitoring appropriate but not most critical for this interaction.
B. CORRECT - P-gp inhibition increases digoxin levels 50-100%; toxicity risk high.
C. BNP useful for HF but not primary concern for this drug interaction.
D. QTc important but digoxin toxicity from elevated levels is more immediate concern.

q: Patient on phenytoin given stat IV diazepam for breakthrough seizures, then started on fluconazole for candidiasis. Most clinically significant concern?
opts: A. Diazepam inhibits phenytoin metabolism, causing toxicity
B. Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9/CYP2C19, increasing phenytoin levels and toxicity risk
C. Phenytoin induces fluconazole metabolism, reducing antifungal efficacy
D. All three drugs synergistically prolong QTc
exp: Phenytoin metabolized by CYP2C9 and CYP2C19. Fluconazole inhibits both enzymes, significantly increasing phenytoin levels. Phenytoin has narrow TI and zero-order kinetics. Small clearance changes cause large unpredictable level increases and toxicity.
opts_exp: A. Diazepam doesn't significantly inhibit phenytoin metabolism; single IV dose is routine.
B. CORRECT - Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9/2C19; phenytoin zero-order kinetics.
C. Phenytoin more commonly reduces fluconazole levels slightly.
D. Phenytoin at therapeutic levels can shorten, not prolong QTc.

q: Which combination requires dose reduction of the substrate due to P-glycoprotein inhibition?
opts: A. Metformin + rifampin
B. Dabigatran + dronedarone
C. Warfarin + phenobarbital
D. Metoprolol + spironolactone
exp: Dabigatran is P-glycoprotein substrate. Dronedarone is potent P-gp inhibitor. Co-administration increases dabigatran AUC 70-100%, significantly raising bleeding risk. Dose should be reduced or combination avoided.
opts_exp: A. Rifampin induces P-gp and would decrease metformin slightly.
B. CORRECT - Dabigatran is P-gp substrate; dronedarone inhibits P-gp strongly.
C. Phenobarbital is CYP inducer, not P-gp inhibitor.
D. No P-gp interaction between these drugs.

q: Patient on fluoxetine for depression starts linezolid for MRSA infection. Key concern?
opts: A. Fluoxetine induces CYP3A4, decreasing linezolid levels
B. Linezolid is weak, reversible non-selective MAO inhibitor; serotonin syndrome risk with SSRIs
C. Fluoxetine blocks linezolid absorption via chelation
D. Both drugs synergistically prolong QTc
exp: Linezolid has weak, reversible MAO inhibitor activity. Combined with serotonergic drugs (SSRIs like fluoxetine), risk of serotonin syndrome. Fluoxetine should ideally be held during linezolid course (5-14 days).
opts_exp: A. Fluoxetine doesn't induce CYP3A4.
B. CORRECT - Linezolid's MAO activity combined with SSRI causes serotonin syndrome.
C. No absorption chelation interaction.
D. Neither significantly prolongs QTc.

q: Patient on apixaban also taking strong CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitor ketoconazole. Effect?
opts: A. No effect - apixaban renally eliminated
