q: A patient with HIV (CD4 45 cells/µL, on prophylaxis with TMP-SMX) develops cryptococcal meningitis confirmed by CSF India ink and cryptococcal antigen. LP opening pressure is 42 cmH₂O. Which initial treatment plan is MOST appropriate?
opts: A. Fluconazole 800mg/day x2 weeks then 400mg/day consolidation
B. Liposomal amphotericin B 3-4mg/kg/day + flucytosine 25mg/kg q6h x2 weeks; serial therapeutic LPs
C. Amphotericin B deoxycholate 0.7-1mg/kg/day + fluconazole 400mg/day x2 weeks
D. Voriconazole 4mg/kg q12h (cryptococcal CSF penetration >90%)
exp: IDSA/WHO guidelines recommend induction with liposomal amphotericin B 3-4 mg/kg/day + flucytosine 25 mg/kg q6h (5-FC) for 2 weeks for HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis. Serial therapeutic LPs are critical to manage elevated ICP (opening pressure >25 cm H₂O — here 42 cmH₂O). Elevated ICP is the leading cause of early mortality. Fluconazole monotherapy is inferior. Amphotericin B deoxycholate is an alternative in resource-limited settings but more nephrotoxic.
ref: ASHP Vol 1; IDSA 2010 Cryptococcal Meningitis Guidelines

q: A 68-year-old man with HFrEF (EF 22%), CKD Stage 3b (eGFR 32), and potassium 5.3 mEq/L is on sacubitril/valsartan 97/103mg BID, metoprolol succinate 200mg daily, eplerenone 25mg daily. His NT-proBNP is 4800 pg/mL. What next step optimizes guideline-directed therapy?
opts: A. Add ivabradine if HR >70 bpm and still in sinus rhythm
B. Increase eplerenone to 50mg — MRA dose titration improves outcomes
C. Discontinue eplerenone (hyperkalemia risk with eGFR <45) and initiate SGLT2 inhibitor
D. Start hydralazine-isosorbide dinitrate — additonal vasodilation with ARNI
exp: Ivabradine (SHIFT trial) reduces cardiovascular death/HF hospitalization in HFrEF patients with EF ≤35%, sinus rhythm, and HR ≥70 bpm on maximally tolerated beta-blocker. This patient is on ARNI + BB + MRA (3 pillars). The fourth pillar per 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA guidelines is SGLT2 inhibitor (option C has merit) but K+ is already 5.3 mEq/L, making increasing eplerenone risky. Ivabradine is the next appropriate GDMT addition if HR criterion is met. SGLT2i would be added simultaneously.

q: A patient undergoes PCI for NSTEMI and receives ticagrelor 90mg BID + aspirin 81mg. Six months post-PCI, she needs elective hip replacement surgery. Her cardiologist says she must have DAPT for 12 months. The surgeon insists on stopping all antiplatelets. What is the best bridging approach?
opts: A. Switch to clopidogrel 5 days pre-op (less bleeding, maintains P2Y12 inhibition)
B. Continue ticagrelor through surgery — bleeding risk acceptable for major orthopedic surgery
C. Stop ticagrelor 3-5 days pre-op (vs 5-7 for clopidogrel), continue aspirin, restart ticagrelor within 24h post-op
D. Switch to IV cangrelor as a bridge for the 3 days before surgery
exp: For patients needing surgery during mandatory DAPT period: Stop ticagrelor 3-5 days before surgery (shorter offset vs. clopidogrel 5-7 days due to shorter half-life). Continue aspirin 81mg through surgery unless neurosurgery/spinal procedure. Restart ticagrelor within 24-48h post-op when hemostasis is achieved. Cangrelor bridging is investigational and not standard practice for elective surgery. Switching to clopidogrel doesn't actually help since both require discontinuation pre-op; however it's not wrong per se.
ref: ASHP Vol 1; ACC/AHA Perioperative DAPT Guidance

q: A patient with mechanical mitral valve (bileaflet) is on warfarin (target INR 2.5-3.5). She develops INR 9.2 with minor bruising and gingival bleeding but no major hemorrhage. She had a supratherapeutic INR because her provider started clarithromycin. What is the most appropriate management?
opts: A. Hold warfarin, administer vitamin K 10mg IV for rapid reversal
B. Hold warfarin, administer oral vitamin K 1-2.5mg, recheck INR in 24-48 hours
C. Hold warfarin only — INR will self-correct within 3-5 days
D. Administer 4F-PCC and vitamin K 10mg IV for immediate reversal
exp: For INR >8 with minor bleeding in a mechanical valve patient: Hold warfarin and administer LOW-dose oral vitamin K (1-2.5mg) to gently lower INR without over-correcting. Over-correction (high-dose IV vitamin K) risks thrombosis of the mechanical valve. 4F-PCC is reserved for life-threatening bleeding. Holding alone (option C) is too slow given INR 9.2. Oral vitamin K 1-2.5mg provides controlled reversal within 24-48h.

q: A 78-year-old male with AF and CrCl 19 mL/min (Cockcroft-Gault) is currently on apixaban 5mg BID. His cardiologist orders a renal panel: SCr 3.4 mg/dL, age 78, weight 62 kg. Which dose adjustment is correct per FDA labeling for apixaban?
opts: A. No change needed — apixaban dose adjustment criteria use TWO of three (age ≥80, SCr ≥1.5, weight ≤60 kg)
B. Reduce to apixaban 2.5mg BID — CrCl <25 mL/min triggers automatic dose reduction
C. Discontinue apixaban; ESRD patients require warfarin only
D. Reduce to apixaban 2.5mg BID because 2 of 3 criteria are met (age ≥80 no; SCr ≥1.5 YES; weight ≤60 kg no)
exp: FDA apixaban dose reduction for AF: Use 2.5mg BID if patient meets ≥2 of: (1) age ≥80, (2) body weight ≤60 kg, (3) SCr ≥1.5 mg/dL. This patient: SCr 3.4 ≥1.5 ✓; weight 62 kg > 60 ✗; age 78 < 80 ✗ → only 1 criterion met → dose remains 5mg BID per labeling. However, this patient has severe CKD (CrCl 19 mL/min) which is a concern for accumulation, and clinical judgment may favor monitoring. BUT strictly per FDA labeling, only 1 criterion is met, so no reduction is indicated. Option D's reasoning is correct (only 1 criterion met, no reduction per FDA) — BUT the conclusion states 2.5mg BID which is incorrect for 1 criterion. CORRECT answer: A.
ref: ASHP Vol 1; Apixaban prescribing information; DOAC renal dosing

q: A 52-year-old woman with T2DM and HFrEF (EF 35%) presents with A1c 9.2%. She is on metformin 1000mg BID, sitagliptin 100mg daily. eGFR is 52 mL/min/1.73m². What addition best addresses both glucose control AND her cardiovascular/HF risk?
opts: A. Liraglutide 1.8mg SC daily — LEADER trial mortality benefit in T2DM + CVD
B. Empagliflozin 10mg daily — EMPA-REG OUTCOME and EMPEROR-Reduced trials show HF hospitalization + mortality benefit
C. Insulin glargine 0.2 U/kg/day — provides better A1c reduction than oral agents
D. Pioglitazone 30mg daily — improves insulin sensitivity and reduces MACE
exp: SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) have demonstrated both glycemic benefit AND significant reductions in HF hospitalization and cardiovascular mortality in patients with HFrEF (EMPEROR-Reduced, DAPA-HF). Empagliflozin is approved for HFrEF regardless of diabetes status. Liraglutide (GLP-1 RA) has cardiovascular benefit but actually showed increased HF hospitalization in some HF trials and is not recommended in HFrEF. Pioglitazone is CONTRAINDICATED in HF due to fluid retention.
ref: ASHP Vol 2, Endocrine; ADA Standards of Medical Care 2024; EMPEROR-Reduced

q: A patient with type 1 diabetes on insulin pump therapy presents with DKA (pH 7.18, glucose 520 mg/dL, bicarb 10 mEq/L, anion gap 28). Potassium is 3.2 mEq/L. What is the correct insulin and potassium management?
opts: A. Start insulin drip immediately at 0.1 U/kg/hr; add KCl to fluids when K+ <3.5
B. Hold insulin drip until potassium ≥3.5 mEq/L; replace K+ aggressively first, then start insulin
C. Start insulin at 0.1 U/kg/hr AND give KCl 20-40 mEq/hr simultaneously given hypokalemia
D. Use subcutaneous insulin correction doses since pump failure likely caused DKA
exp: DKA protocol: If K+ <3.5 mEq/L, HOLD insulin and replace potassium FIRST (KCl 20-40 mEq/hr IV) until K+ ≥3.5 before starting insulin. This prevents life-threatening hypokalemia — insulin drives K+ intracellularly and can precipitate fatal arrhythmias if started with K+ <3.5. Once K+ ≥3.5, start insulin 0.1 U/kg/hr. Add KCl 20-30 mEq/L to IV fluids when K+ <5.0 to maintain K+ 4-5 mEq/L during treatment.
ref: ASHP Vol 2; ADA DKA Management Guidelines

q: A 67-year-old with COPD (GOLD Group E — 3 exacerbations last year, CAT score 28, FEV₁ 38% predicted) is on LABA/LAMA (tiotropium + salmeterol). He also has an eosinophil count of 420 cells/µL. Which treatment modification is supported by evidence to reduce exacerbations?
opts: A. Add roflumilast 500 mcg daily — PDE4 inhibitor reduces exacerbations in chronic bronchitis phenotype
B. Add ICS to form triple therapy (LABA/LAMA/ICS) — IMPACT trial shows reduction in exacerbations with eos ≥150
C. Switch to azithromycin 250mg daily for exacerbation prophylaxis
D. Both A and B are appropriate add-on options with evidence for this patient
exp: Both options have evidence for GOLD Group E patients: (1) ICS addition (LABA/LAMA/ICS triple therapy) — IMPACT trial showed significant reduction in moderate/severe exacerbations with eosinophils ≥150 cells/µL; this patient has 420 → strong candidate. (2) Roflumilast — indicated for severe COPD with chronic bronchitis and frequent exacerbations (FEV₁ <50% + chronic bronchitis phenotype). Given FEV₁ 38% and 3 exacerbations, both are guideline-supported additions. GOLD 2023/2024 supports this dual approach.
ref: ASHP Vol 2, Pulmonology; GOLD 2024 Report; IMPACT trial

q: A 44-year-old woman is diagnosed with pulmonary arterial hypertension (WHO Group 1, WHO FC III). She has no contraindications. echocardiography shows severely reduced RV function. What combination therapy is recommended for this risk level?
opts: A. Sildenafil monotherapy — first-line for WHO FC III PAH
B. Ambrisentan + tadalafil (AMBITION trial) — upfront combination has superior outcomes vs monotherapy
C. IV epoprostenol + bosentan — only option for severe FC III disease
D. Subcutaneous treprostinil + sildenafil — parenteral prostacyclin required for FC III
exp: The AMBITION trial demonstrated that upfront combination therapy with ambrisentan (ERA) + tadalafil (PDE5i) significantly reduced the risk of clinical failure events compared to either monotherapy in WHO FC II/III PAH patients. This is now the preferred initial strategy for intermediate-to-high risk PAH per ACC/AHA/ERS guidelines. IV epoprostenol is reserved for high-risk/FC IV patients or those failing oral therapy.
ref: ASHP Vol 2; AMBITION Trial; 2022 ESC/ERS PAH Guidelines

q: A 58-year-old woman with CKD Stage 4 (eGFR 28) and type 2 diabetes has albumin-to-creatinine ratio 1,800 mg/g on maximal ACE inhibitor therapy (lisinopril 40mg/day, K+ 4.8 mEq/L). She is not on an MRA. Blood pressure is 138/88 despite 3 antihypertensives. Which new agent most strongly reduces CKD progression?
opts: A. Spironolactone 25mg daily — MRA provides additional proteinuria reduction
B. Finerenone 10-20mg daily — nonsteroidal MRA reduces CKD progression and CV events in T2DM with CKD (FIDELIO-DKD)
C. Empagliflozin 10mg daily — EMPA-KIDNEY/CREDENCE trials show nephroprotection
D. Both B and C are recommended; evidence supports combination in CKD + T2DM
exp: Both finerenone and SGLT2 inhibitors have strong evidence for nephroprotection in T2DM + CKD: Finerenone (FIDELIO-DKD, FIGARO-DKD) reduces composite kidney/CV outcomes. SGLT2 inhibitors (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY) reduce kidney disease progression independent of glycemia. 2024 ADA/KDIGO guidelines recommend BOTH classes (on top of RAS blockade) for T2DM with CKD when tolerated. Spironolactone (option A) has less evidence and more hyperkalemia risk in advanced CKD than finerenone.
ref: ASHP Vol 2, Nephrology; KDIGO 2022 CKD Guidelines; FIDELIO-DKD

q: A 28-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder becomes pregnant (6 weeks). She is on lithium 900mg/day (level 0.8 mEq/L) and lamotrigine 200mg/day. Which medication management is most appropriate given the teratogenicity profile?
opts: A. Discontinue lithium immediately — Ebstein anomaly risk is absolute contraindication in pregnancy
B. Continue both medications with enhanced fetal monitoring; absolute Ebstein risk with lithium remains low (~1-2 per 1,000 vs ~1 per 20,000 baseline), still <1%; benefit-risk favors continuation
C. Switch lithium to valproate — better evidence in bipolar I during pregnancy
D. Discontinue both medications — all mood stabilizers are category D or X in pregnancy
exp: The decision to continue lithium in pregnancy requires careful benefit-risk analysis. Modern data shows lithium's Ebstein anomaly risk is approximately 1-2 per 1,000 exposures (vs. 1 per 20,000 baseline) — a relative increase but absolute risk remains very low. For a patient with severe bipolar I, discontinuing lithium risks serious mood episodes which carry their own fetal/maternal risks (suicide, preterm delivery). Per ACOG and perinatal psychiatry guidelines: continue lithium with fetal echocardiography at 18-20 weeks, use divided doses to reduce peak levels, and increase monitoring. Valproate has significantly HIGHER teratogenicity (neural tube defects, FASD) and is contraindicated in pregnancy for bipolar.
ref: ASHP Vol 1, Psychiatry; ACOG Bipolar Disorder in Pregnancy Guidelines

q: A 32-year-old male with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has failed 3 adequate antipsychotic trials. Clozapine 300mg/day is initiated. Week 4 ANC is 1,200 cells/mm³ (down from 4,800 baseline). What is the appropriate action per REMS guidelines?
opts: A. Continue clozapine — mild neutropenia expected; recheck in 1 week
B. Reduce clozapine dose by 50% and recheck ANC in 3 days
C. Interrupt clozapine; ANC <1,500 triggers hold; recheck ANC every day until ≥1,500, then every week x4 weeks
D. Permanently discontinue clozapine; ANC <1,500 is an absolute contraindication to re-challenge
exp: Per current FDA clozapine ANC monitoring: ANC 1,000–1,499 = MILD neutropenia → CONTINUE clozapine and monitor ANC 3× weekly until ≥1,500. Interrupt only at ANC 500–999 (moderate; daily ANC until ≥1,000) and discontinue/do not rechallenge at <500 (severe/agranulocytosis). This patient’s ANC 1,200 is mild → continue (option A). Note: the FDA removed the Clozapine REMS in Feb 2025, but these ANC thresholds remain in the prescribing information.
ref: ASHP Vol 1; FDA Clozapine REMS Guidelines 2021

q: A 34-year-old woman with relapsing-remitting MS (2 relapses/year, 5 new T2 lesions) is on interferon beta-1a and wants to switch to a higher-efficacy agent. She plans pregnancy in 2 years. Which agent provides high efficacy with an acceptable reproductive safety profile?
opts: A. Natalizumab — high efficacy, but PML risk and must stop ≥3 months before conception
B. Alemtuzumab — durable remission after 2 treatment courses; teratogenicity risk low after washout
C. Ocrelizumab — B-cell depletion; extensive washout required (6-12 months) before conception
D. Cladribine — oral, high efficacy, 2-year treatment course; contraception required only during treatment and 6 months after last dose
exp: For RRMS patients planning pregnancy, cladribine tablets offer: high efficacy (similar to alemtuzumab in trials), a 2-year oral treatment course, and a defined 6-month contraception window after the last dose — then pregnancy is acceptable. Natalizumab requires 3-month washout minimum and has neonatal hematologic issues with perinatal exposure. Alemtuzumab has long duration but thyroid autoimmunity risk and complex monitoring. Ocrelizumab requires 6-12 month washout with uncertain fetal B-cell depletion effects.
ref: ASHP Vol 1, Neurology; EAN/ECTRIMS MS Pregnancy Guidelines

q: A 72-year-old male with acute ischemic stroke (NIHSS 18, large vessel occlusion) receives IV alteplase at 2.5 hours and undergoes thrombectomy at 4.8 hours. His blood pressure is 210/115 mmHg post-thrombolysis. What is the BP target and optimal agent?
opts: A. Target <180/105 mmHg using IV labetalol or nicardipine; avoid excessive lowering
B. Target <160/90 mmHg aggressively — reduces hemorrhagic transformation risk
C. No BP treatment needed — autoregulation maintains perfusion in penumbra
D. Target MAP <130 mmHg using IV clevidipine — most titratable option
exp: Post-IV thrombolysis, the BP target is <180/105 mmHg per AHA/ASA stroke guidelines. More aggressive lowering risks hypoperfusion of the ischemic penumbra. First-line agents: IV labetalol (10-20mg over 1-2 min; max 300mg) or IV nicardipine (5-15 mg/hr continuous infusion). Clevidipine is also acceptable but not specifically preferred over nicardipine. Nitroprusside/nitrates should be avoided (cerebral vasodilation increases ICP).

q: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer progresses on pertuzumab + trastuzumab + docetaxel after 8 cycles. ECOG PS 1. What second-line therapy has the strongest overall survival evidence?
opts: A. Lapatinib + capecitabine (EGF100151 trial)
B. Trastuzumab emtansine (T-DM1/ado-trastuzumab emtansine) — EMILIA trial OS benefit
C. Trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) — DESTINY-Breast03 showed superior PFS vs T-DM1
D. Neratinib + capecitabine — NALA trial benefit
exp: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd, DS-8201) — DESTINY-Breast03 trial showed significantly superior PFS and OS compared to T-DM1 in HER2+ mBC after progression on trastuzumab-based therapy, establishing T-DXd as the preferred second-line treatment. Key safety consideration: T-DXd has a risk of interstitial lung disease (ILD)/pneumonitis (~10-15%, ~1% Grade 5) requiring monitoring. T-DM1 (option B) was previously the standard of care but is now second-line after T-DXd.
ref: ASHP Vol 2, Oncology; DESTINY-Breast03; NCCN Breast Cancer v2.2024

q: A 65-year-old male receives CHOP-R (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisone) for DLBCL. After cycle 2, he develops numbness and tingling in fingers/toes, bilateral foot drop. Vincristine-induced peripheral neuropathy (VIPN) is suspected. What management is appropriate?
opts: A. Add gabapentin for symptomatic relief; continue vincristine at full dose
B. Delay next cycle until resolution, then reduce vincristine dose by 50%
C. Omit vincristine from next cycle; VIPN with foot drop is motor neuropathy indicating dose discontinuation
D. Switch entire regimen to DA-EPOCH-R which uses continuous vincristine infusion to reduce VIPN
exp: Vincristine-induced peripheral neuropathy (VIPN) grading guides management: Motor neuropathy (foot drop = Grade 3-4) is an indication to discontinue vincristine. Foot drop/functional impairment is a Grade 3+ motor toxicity requiring omission of vincristine from subsequent cycles per NCCN and institutional guidelines. Dose reduction alone is insufficient for motor neuropathy with functional loss. DA-EPOCH-R uses continuous infusion vincristine but switching regimens at this point is not indicated.
ref: ASHP Vol 2; NCCN B-cell Lymphomas; Vincristine toxicity management

q: An 84-year-old male with moderate dementia, HTN, AF, CKD 3 (CrCl 38), and insomnia is brought by family who report new confusion. His medications include lisinopril, metoprolol, apixaban 2.5mg BID, diphenhydramine 50mg nightly (OTC), and donepezil 10mg. What is the most likely cause of his worsening confusion and appropriate change?
opts: A. Donepezil causing cholinergic excess — reduce dose to 5mg
B. Apixaban causing microhemorrhages — hold and order brain MRI
C. Diphenhydramine (Beers Criteria) — anticholinergic effect worsens dementia/cognition; discontinue immediately
D. Metoprolol causing CNS depression in the elderly — switch to carvedilol
exp: Diphenhydramine is on the Beers Criteria as a 'strong anticholinergic' agent that is explicitly contraindicated in older adults — especially those with dementia, where it markedly worsens cognition, causes confusion, hallucinations, falls, and urinary retention. Anticholinergic burden is a well-established risk in geriatric patients with dementia. Donepezil (cholinesterase inhibitor) would counteract anticholinergic effects. The first intervention should be immediate discontinuation of diphenhydramine. Alternatives: melatonin, low-dose doxepin, or CBT-I.
ref: ASHP Vol 2, Geriatrics; AGS 2023 Beers Criteria

q: A 4-year-old child (18 kg) presents with acute status epilepticus. Seizure has been continuous for 8 minutes. IV access is unavailable. What is the first-line treatment?
opts: A. Rectal diazepam 0.5 mg/kg — established first-line when IV access unavailable
B. Intranasal midazolam 0.2 mg/kg (max 5mg per nostril) — equivalent to IV and faster than rectal
C. IM lorazepam 0.05 mg/kg — reliable absorption; preferred per pediatric guidelines
D. Buccal midazolam 0.5 mg/kg — established non-IV benzodiazepine for pediatric SE
exp: For pediatric status epilepticus without IV access, intranasal (IN) midazolam 0.2 mg/kg (max 10mg total; split between nostrils if >5mg) is recommended first-line by pediatric neurology and emergency medicine guidelines — non-inferior to IV lorazepam (LoSE trial), faster onset than rectal diazepam, and more practical. Buccal midazolam is also used but IN is preferred in the acute ED/prehospital setting. Rectal diazepam is used in community/home settings but less practical in ED. IM lorazepam is appropriate when IN unavailable.
ref: ASHP Vol 2, Pediatrics; Pediatric SE Management Guidelines

q: A 52-year-old man with cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B) and large ascites presents with spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) confirmed by PMN count >250/mm³ in peritoneal fluid. What is the correct treatment including prevention of hepatorenal syndrome?
opts: A. Cefotaxime 2g IV q8h alone — adequate antibiotic coverage for SBP
B. Cefotaxime 2g IV q8h + albumin 1.5 g/kg IV on Day 1 and 1.0 g/kg on Day 3 — albumin prevents HRS
C. Ciprofloxacin 400mg IV q12h + albumin 1.5 g/kg IV — quinolone equally effective as cephalosporin
D. Ceftriaxone 1g IV q24h alone — sufficient for outpatient treatment of mild SBP
exp: SBP treatment: Cefotaxime 2g IV q8h (or ceftriaxone 1g q24h) is first-line antibiotic. The key additional intervention is albumin infusion — IV albumin 1.5 g/kg on Day 1 + 1.0 g/kg on Day 3 — proven in the Sort et al. NEJM 1999 trial to significantly reduce the incidence of hepatorenal syndrome (HRS) and mortality in SBP patients with SCr >1 mg/dL, BUN >30 mg/dL, or bilirubin >4 mg/dL. This is a guideline-recommended standard of care. SBP is not appropriate for outpatient management in Child-Pugh B/C patients.
ref: ASHP Vol 2, GI; AASLD SBP Guidelines; Sort et al. NEJM 1999

q: A 44-year-old woman with Crohn's disease (perianal fistulizing, on azathioprine) has active disease despite 3 months of therapy. Infliximab is planned. Before initiation, which screening tests are MOST critical?
opts: A. CBC with differential and LFTs only — monitor for azathioprine toxicity
B. TB screening (IGRA or TST), hepatitis B serology (HBsAg, anti-HBc), and update live vaccines before starting
C. TB screening, hepatitis C antibody, and HIV test
D. Only IGRA for TB — other infections are not significantly reactivated by TNF inhibitors
exp: Before initiating TNF inhibitor (infliximab): (1) Screen for LATENT TB — IGRA (preferred) or TST; if positive, treat latent TB before starting biologics. (2) Screen for hepatitis B — HBsAg and anti-HBc; reactivation is a boxed warning. (3) Ensure vaccinations are up to date, specifically ALL live vaccines (MMR, varicella, zoster shingles) must be given BEFORE initiating because they are contraindicated on biologics. HCV screening is recommended but less critical. HIV screening is also important.
ref: ASHP Vol 2; ACG Crohn's Disease Guidelines; TNFi pre-screening

q: A randomized trial of a new antibiotic vs placebo reports: OR 2.4 (95% CI 1.1–5.2, p=0.03) for clinical cure. An accompanying meta-analysis shows OR 1.8 (95% CI 0.92–3.5, p=0.09) for the same outcome across 5 trials (I² = 67%). How should you interpret these discordant results?
opts: A. The individual trial is definitive; meta-analysis likely underpowered with high heterogeneity
B. The meta-analysis supersedes the single trial; the therapy likely has no significant effect
C. High heterogeneity (I²=67%) makes meta-analysis results unreliable; neither result is definitive; clinical context needed
D. Both results show benefit (OR >1); the difference in significance is purely due to sample size
exp: I² = 67% indicates substantial heterogeneity — the variation in results across studies is not due to chance. When I² is high (>50-75%), pooling results in a meta-analysis is questionable and the summary OR may be misleading. The discordance (single trial significant, meta-analysis not) could reflect: (1) heterogeneous patient populations across trials, (2) different drug doses or durations, or (3) true inconsistency. The correct interpretation is that neither result is definitive and the clinical context/sources of heterogeneity must be explored. Publication bias and subgroup analyses should also be considered.
ref: ASHP Vol 2, Biostatistics; Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews

q: A pharmacoeconomic analysis of a new hepatitis C drug claims: ICER = $42,000/QALY (base case). The report states the result is 'robust' based on a deterministic sensitivity analysis varying costs ±25%. A reviewer argues this is insufficient. What additional analysis is needed?
opts: A. Budget impact analysis (BIA) — examines affordability at population level
B. Probabilistic sensitivity analysis (PSA) — Monte Carlo simulation to quantify parameter uncertainty simultaneously across all variables
C. Threshold analysis — identifies at what price the drug crosses the willingness-to-pay threshold
D. Subgroup analysis stratified by genotype — heterogeneity in treatment effect affects ICER
exp: Deterministic (one-way or two-way) sensitivity analysis tests model robustness by varying one or two parameters at a time. It does NOT capture the joint uncertainty across all model parameters simultaneously. Probabilistic Sensitivity Analysis (PSA) uses Monte Carlo simulation to sample from probability distributions for all input parameters simultaneously, generating a cost-effectiveness acceptability curve (CEAC) and confidence intervals around the ICER. PSA is the gold standard for quantifying model uncertainty in CEAs. BIA is separate (affordability, not cost-effectiveness). Threshold analysis is useful but less comprehensive than PSA.
ref: ASHP Vol 2, Pharmacoeconomics; ISPOR Guidelines for Economic Modeling

q: A pharmacist receives a complex drug information question about a novel oncology agent. The tertiary databases (Micromedex, Lexicomp) have no data. The package insert mentions one phase I dose-escalation study (n=12). What is the appropriate evidence hierarchy approach?
opts: A. Use the package insert data; it represents the most up-to-date manufacturer information
B. Search PubMed for primary literature, then conference abstracts; acknowledge quality limitations
C. Contact the manufacturer's medical information line as primary source, then search PubMed for independent data
D. Declare insufficient evidence and recommend standard of care without the new agent
exp: For novel agents with limited tertiary database coverage, the pharmacist should: (1) Search PubMed/MEDLINE for primary peer-reviewed literature, including phase I-III trial data. (2) Search conference proceedings (ASCO, ASH, ESMO) for unpublished abstracts. (3) Consider manufacturer medical information as a supplementary source (often has unpublished data) but not as a primary independent source. The package insert reflects FDA-approved labeling and may omit relevant safety/efficacy data discovered post-approval or from off-label use. All sources should be cited with quality assessment.
ref: ASHP Vol 2, Drug Information Resources; Malone et al. Drug Information Textbook

q: A hospital pharmacy is implementing a pharmacist-led antimicrobial stewardship program (ASP). The program wants to measure process and outcome metrics. Which metric pair BEST reflects both process and outcome?
opts: A. Days of therapy (DOT)/1000 patient-days AND C. difficile incidence per 10,000 patient-days
B. Number of interventions documented AND total cost savings
C. Percentage of restricted antibiotic approvals AND length of stay
D. Time to antibiotic therapy AND 30-day readmission rates
exp: IDSA/SHEA ASP guidelines recommend both PROCESS metrics (what the program does) and OUTCOME metrics (what happens to patients/institution): Process: Days of Therapy (DOT)/1,000 patient-days (standardizes antibiotic use independent of census) — directly measures ASP intervention impact. Outcome: C. difficile infection rate per 10,000 patient-days — a sentinel event reflecting selective antibiotic pressure on the gut microbiome. Together these measure stewardship activity AND its clinical impact. The pairing in option A is the standard endorsed by IDSA/SHEA.
ref: ASHP Vol 2; IDSA/SHEA Antimicrobial Stewardship Guidelines 2016

q: A pharmacy director is evaluating a formulary request for a biosimilar to adalimumab. The biosimilar has an FDA designation as 'interchangeable.' What does interchangeable designation mean, and what authority does the pharmacist have?
opts: A. Interchangeable means clinically equivalent; pharmacist may substitute without prescriber notification in all 50 states
B. Interchangeable designation means pharmacist CAN substitute without prescriber consent per FDA, but state law governs actual substitution authority — must check state pharmacy practice act
C. Interchangeable means the biosimilar was proven equivalent in a switching study; substitution requires prescriber approval in all cases
D. Interchangeable designation does not exist for monoclonal antibodies; all biologics require prescriber authorization
exp: FDA 'interchangeable' designation means the biosimilar was demonstrated to produce the same clinical result AND that switching between the reference and biosimilar does not increase safety/efficacy concerns (switching studies). This meets the standard for pharmacist substitution WITHOUT prescriber notification at the federal level. HOWEVER, state pharmacy practice acts govern whether substitution is actually permitted — most states allow substitution with interchangeable biosimilars, but laws vary (notification requirements differ). Pharmacists must know their state's requirements.
ref: ASHP Vol 2; FDA Biosimilar Guidance; NABP Model Pharmacy Practice Act

q: A landmark trial is criticized because 28% of patients randomized to placebo crossed over to the active drug arm at week 8 due to disease progression. The primary endpoint (ITT analysis) showed HR=0.72 (p=0.04) favoring the drug. A per-protocol analysis shows HR=0.61 (p=0.001). How does crossover affect interpretation?
opts: A. ITT analysis underestimates the true treatment effect because crossover dilutes the comparison
B. Per-protocol analysis should be used as primary since it captures actual drug exposure
C. Both analyses are biased; only rank-preserved structural nested models can correct for crossover
D. Crossover only affects safety analysis; efficacy is unaffected in randomized trials
exp: High crossover from control to active arm biases ITT analysis TOWARD THE NULL (underestimates true treatment effect) because control patients eventually receive the active drug, making outcomes in both arms more similar. This is why the ITT HR (0.72) is less pronounced than per-protocol (0.61). However, per-protocol analysis introduces selection bias (patients remaining on treatment may differ systematically). Statistical approaches like rank-preserved structural failure time models (RPSFTM) or two-stage adjustment can better estimate the true treatment effect. The ITT remains the primary analysis but must be interpreted in context of crossover.
ref: ASHP Vol 2, Study Design; CONSORT; Crossover adjustment statistical methods

q: A non-inferiority trial of oral sotalol vs IV amiodarone for AF cardioversion was conducted with a non-inferiority margin of 10%. The placebo-controlled historical data showed amiodarone success rate = 65%. Oral sotalol success rate = 58%. The 95% CI for the difference is -10% to +3%. What conclusion is correct?
opts: A. Sotalol is non-inferior — the CI upper bound (+3%) is within the margin
B. Sotalol is non-inferior — the lower bound (-10%) does not exceed the -10% non-inferiority margin
C. Results are inconclusive — the confidence interval just touches the non-inferiority margin at -10%, which does not establish non-inferiority
D. Sotalol is inferior — the point estimate shows lower success rate (58% vs 65%)
exp: Non-inferiority (NI) is established when the ENTIRE 95% CI falls within the NI margin. Here the NI margin is -10% (sotalol can be at most 10% worse than amiodarone). The CI lower bound is exactly -10% — this JUST touches the boundary. Non-inferiority requires the lower confidence bound to be STRICTLY within (better than) the NI margin, not equal to it. Therefore, non-inferiority is not demonstrated. This is a boundary case where the trial is formally inconclusive. In practice, most regulators would NOT accept this as establishing NI.
ref: ASHP Vol 2; CONSORT NI Trial Reporting; FDA NI Guidance 2016

q: A drug has a Ki of 2 nM at CYP3A4. At steady-state plasma concentrations of 50 nM, what is the expected fold-increase in exposure ([I]/Ki + 1) for a substrate where this is the sole clearance pathway?
opts: A. 24-fold increase in AUC for the substrate
B. 26-fold increase in AUC for the substrate
C. 52-fold increase in AUC for the substrate
D. 50-fold increase in AUC for the substrate
exp: Using the reversible inhibition equation: AUC ratio = 1 / (1 - (I/(I+Ki))) or for competitive inhibition AUC ratio = 1 + I/Ki (simplified for inhibitor effect on clearance via the Rowland-Matin equation when fm=1). If [I]/Ki = 50/2 = 25, then the inhibition quotient = 25+1 = 26 (the substrate's AUC is expected to increase approximately 26-fold). This is the [I]/Ki + 1 approach used in FDA drug interaction guidance. FDA considers [I]/Ki ≥ 0.1 a positive signal for in vitro DDI studies, and ≥ 25 signals a very potent inhibitor.
ref: ASHP Vol 2; FDA Drug Interaction Guidance; Rowland-Matin equation

q: A 26-year-old woman is prescribed doxycycline 100mg BID for 7 days for Chlamydia infection. She is also on combined oral contraceptives (OCP). She asks whether the antibiotic affects OCP efficacy. What is the evidence-based counseling?
opts: A. Yes, doxycycline significantly reduces OCP efficacy via gut flora disruption; use backup contraception during and 7 days after
B. No significant pharmacokinetic interaction — doxycycline does not affect CYP3A4 or gut-flora mediated OCP enterohepatic cycling in studies; backup contraception not required
C. Doxycycline is a CYP3A4 inducer; reduces ethinyl estradiol levels by 40%; requires backup method
D. Only rifampin and anticonvulsants affect OCP; all other antibiotics are safe
exp: Current evidence (multiple PK studies) does NOT support a clinically meaningful reduction in OCP efficacy with most antibiotics (including doxycycline, amoxicillin, ampicillin). The theoretical gut-flora disruption hypothesis has not been confirmed in clinical studies. ACOG, WHO, CDC, and major contraception guidelines updated their guidance: routine backup contraception is NOT required with non-enzyme-inducing antibiotics. The exceptions that DO require backup: enzyme-inducing drugs (rifampin/rifabutin, certain anticonvulsants like phenytoin/carbamazepine).
ref: ASHP Vol 1; ACOG Practice Bulletin; CDC Medical Eligibility Criteria

q: A kidney transplant recipient (1 year post-transplant) on tacrolimus + mycophenolate + prednisone develops CMV disease (colitis, viral load 12,000 IU/mL). He has eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m². Which antiviral regimen and monitoring is most appropriate?
opts: A. IV ganciclovir 5mg/kg q12h (adjust for eGFR) until asymptomatic and viral load undetectable
B. Oral valganciclovir 900mg BID (adjust dose for CrCl <60 → 450mg BID) plus immunosuppression reduction; monitor weekly CMV VL
C. Foscarnet IV — preferred in CMV colitis due to better GI tissue penetration
D. IV cidofovir 5mg/kg weekly — antiviral drug of choice for CMV disease in transplant
exp: CMV disease in solid organ transplant: Oral valganciclovir (the prodrug of ganciclovir) is preferred for non-life-threatening CMV disease (colitis, viremia) when patient can take oral medications. Dose-adjust for renal function: CrCl 25-59 mL/min → 450mg BID (instead of 900mg BID). Concomitant reduction in immunosuppression (reduce MMF/tacrolimus) aids viral clearance. Monitor CMV PCR weekly until 2 consecutive undetectable values. IV ganciclovir is used for severe disease/poor GI absorption. Foscarnet is for ganciclovir-resistant CMV. Cidofovir is nephrotoxic and reserved for resistant cases.
ref: ASHP Vol 1; AST/IDSA CMV Guidelines; Transplant ID

q: A 68-year-old woman is on heparin infusion post-mechanical valve replacement. On day 7, platelet count drops from 220,000 to 54,000. 4T score is calculated: Thrombocytopenia >50% (2), timing day 5-10 (2), thrombosis none (0), other cause unlikely (1) = 5/8. What is the appropriate management?
opts: A. Stop heparin, begin direct thrombin inhibitor (argatroban or bivalirudin); check HIT antibody (SRA or PF4-ELISA)
B. Continue heparin; thrombocytopenia is expected post-cardiac surgery (HIT-1)
C. Reduce heparin dose by 50% and recheck platelet count in 24 hours
D. Transfuse platelets to maintain >50,000 and continue heparin for valve protection
exp: 4T score 5-8 = HIGH probability of HIT. Management: STOP all heparin products IMMEDIATELY (including heparin flushes, LMWH). Start alternative anticoagulation with a non-heparin agent: argatroban (preferred with normal liver function) or bivalirudin. Send HIT antibody testing (PF4/heparin IgG ELISA for screening; serotonin release assay for confirmation). Do NOT transfuse platelets (may worsen thrombosis). Warfarin should not be started until platelets recover to >150,000 (risk of venous limb gangrene).
ref: ASHP Vol 1; ACCP HIT Management Guidelines

q: A 74-year-old man is admitted to ICU with cardiogenic shock (SBP 78 mmHg, CI 1.6 L/min/m², pulmonary capillary wedge pressure 26 mmHg, SVR 2200 dyn·sec/cm⁵) after anterior STEMI. He is on dopamine 10 mcg/kg/min. Which vasopressor/inotrope change is supported by evidence?
opts: A. Add vasopressin 0.03 units/min to reduce dopamine dose
B. Switch dopamine to norepinephrine + dobutamine — SOAP-II trial: dopamine increases arrhythmia and mortality in CS
C. Increase dopamine to 20 mcg/kg/min — dose-dependent cardiac output improvement
D. Add milrinone 0.375-0.75 mcg/kg/min — PDE3 inhibitor increases CI without vasoconstriction
exp: SOAP-II trial: Dopamine vs. norepinephrine for shock — in the cardiogenic shock subgroup, dopamine was associated with higher 28-day mortality and significantly more arrhythmias. Current guidelines recommend norepinephrine as the vasopressor of choice for cardiogenic shock with hypotension. Adding dobutamine (2.5-20 mcg/kg/min) addresses the low CI (inotropy) while norepinephrine maintains perfusion pressure. Milrinone is an alternative inotrope but causes more hypotension (vasodilation) and is problematic in severe hypotension.
ref: ASHP Vol 1; SOAP-II Trial; AHA Cardiogenic Shock Scientific Statement 2019

q: A 45-year-old alcoholic patient with liver cirrhosis presents with serum sodium 112 mEq/L. He is asymptomatic (no seizures, no altered mental status). Rate of sodium correction for asymptomatic severe hyponatremia is:
opts: A. Correct at 1-2 mEq/L/hr until sodium is 130 mEq/L — prevent cerebral edema
B. Correct no more than 8 mEq/L in the first 24h, 18 mEq/L in 48h — prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS)
C. Correct no more than 10-12 mEq/L in 24h or 18 mEq/L in 48h; for high-risk patients (alcoholism, malnutrition, liver disease) limit to 8 mEq/L/24h
D. Aggressive correction to 125 mEq/L within 4-6 hours is safe for asymptomatic hyponatremia
exp: Correction limits for hyponatremia: Typically no more than 10-12 mEq/L in 24h or 18 mEq/L in 48h to prevent ODS (osmotic demyelination syndrome / central pontine myelinolysis). For HIGH-RISK patients (alcoholism, malnutrition, advanced liver disease, hypokalemia, prior hypoxia) the limit is stricter: ≤8 mEq/L in 24h. This patient is an alcoholic with liver cirrhosis = high risk → 8 mEq/L/24h maximum. Overly rapid correction causes ODS, an irreversible demyelinating injury. If overcorrection occurs, D5W ± desmopressin can re-lower sodium.
ref: ASHP Vol 1; SIADH/Hyponatremia Clinical Practice Guidelines

q: A 27-year-old woman is in her first trimester of pregnancy. She has been diagnosed this week with Graves disease, and her endocrinologist is looking for suggestions on implementing pharmacotherapy to both improve her symptoms (heat intolerance and weight loss) and help normalize her free T4 and thyroid-simulating hormone. Which is the most appropriate answer?
opts: A. Add radioactive iodine
B. Add propanolol
C. Add methimazole
D. Add propylthiouricil
exp: According to current guidelines, propylthiouracil is the recommended treatment of choice during the first trimester because the risk to the fetus is minimal during this time (Answer D is correct). Radioactive iodine is not recommended in pregnancy (Answer A is incorrect). Methimazole would help both normalize her thyroid levels and improve her symptoms but is contraindicated in the first trimester because of increased risk to the fetus (Answer C is incorrect). Though propranolol may help with he

q: A premenopausal patient is experiencing chronic abnormal uterine bleeding. Which therapy is most recommended for the patient at this time?
opts: A. Tranexamic acid
B. Desmopressin
C. Medroxyprogesterone acetate
D. Danazol
exp: Hormonal therapy is recommended as first-line treatment for chronic abnormal uterine bleeding. Medroxyprogesterone acetate, levonorgestrel intrauterine device, and combined hormonal contraceptives are recommended as first-line therapies (Answer C is correct). Tranexamic acid and desmopressin may be used for acute treatment of abnormal uterine bleeding, but they are not recommended for chronic treatment (Answers A and B are incorrect). Danazol may be used in the treatment of endometriosis and is

q: A 16-year-old adolescent (weight: 60 kg) was recently diagnosed with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). The patient's medical history is significant for asthma and tic disorder. The patient endorses the occasional use of alcohol as well as random pills at parties. Which is the best recommendation for this patient for the management of ADHD?
opts: A. Initiate atomoxetine twice daily
B. Initiate methylphenidate sustained twice daily and cognitive behavioral therapy
C. Initiate mixed amphetamine salts extended release once daily
D. Consider behavioral therapy alone
exp: For children 12 years and older, the AAP recommends initial management of ADHD with behavioral treatment together with any ADHD medication that has FDA approval (Answer D is incorrect). Although methylphenidate-containing products and amphetamine-containing products have FDA approval for ADHD and are commonly used first line for ADHD, they would not be ideal agents in an adolescent who endorses illegal substance use (Answers B and C are incorrect). Atomoxetine is not only an agent with FDA appro

q: A 59-year-old man with a history of chronic heart failure, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and osteoarthritis is admitted to the hospital with an exacerbation of heart failure. The admitting physician orders furosemide 40 mg intravenously in addition to the patient's home medications from a recent discharge note in the electronic medical record. The list was as follows: carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily, valsartan 160 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily, clonidine 0.1 mg three times a day, metoprolol 25 mg twice daily, and naproxen 250 mg twice daily. Laboratory results show electrolytes and CBC are WNL (within normal limits), but SCr is 2.1 mg/dL. The patient works as a medical malpractice attorney, drinks two to four alcoholic beverages daily, and is widowed according to the medical record reviewed by the pharmacist. Which characteristic makes this patient most likely to benefit from medication reconciliation?
opts: A. Diagnosis of heart failure
B. Presence of more than three diagnoses
C. Taking more than five medications
D. Drinking more than two alcoholic beverages a day
exp: Patients taking more than five medications have been identified as a priority group for medication reconciliation, and this patient has seven medications from a previous discharge list plus the furosemide ordered on admission (Answer C is correct). Patients who have a diagnosis of renal impairment or a history of transplantation, but not heart failure, are other priority groups (Answer A is incorrect). Number of diagnoses per se has not been identified as a group most likely to benefit from medi

q: T.B. is a 64-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and metabolic dysfunction-associated liver disease (MASLD). The patient is currently taking many medications and wonders if she can do anything naturally to improve her MASLD. Which is the best advice to give this patient?
opts: A. Only resmetirom can improve MASLD
B. She should aim for a goal of at least 7% to 10% weight loss from diet improvement
C. She should limit alcoholic beverages to 2 standard drinks per day
D. She should start taking a natural supplement to cleanse her liver
exp: A goal of at least 7% to 10% weight loss from improving diet and restricting calories is the first-line recommendation for a patient with MASLD (Answer B is correct). Resmetirom can improve MASLD, but this patient is asking for a nonmedication therapy, and not enough information is provided about her. Resmetirom is only approved in stage F2/F3 liver fibrosis (Answer A is incorrect). Patients with MASLD should avoid all alcoholic beverages, and women should at least limit alcohol intake to 1 drin

q: A patient has been diagnosed with neuropathic pain for the past 3 years. Treatment has been unsuccessful with maximally tolerated dosing of amitriptyline and gabapentin. The patient's medical history includes hypertension and atrial fibrillation, for which they take lisinopril 10 mg by mouth daily, verapamil extended release 180 mg by mouth daily, and warfarin 5 mg by mouth daily. The patient's CHA2DS2-VASc score is 5. The patient's INR has been therapeutic for the past 2 years with regular anticoagulation clinic visits. Which treatment would be best for the patient's neuropathic pain?
opts: A. Duloxetine
B. Carbamazepine
C. Topiramate
D. Tramadol
exp: Two maximally tolerated first-line agents for neuropathic pain have failed to alleviate the patient's neuropathic pain. Carbamazepine and topiramate are both third-line treatments for neuropathic pain and would not be best in this case (Answers B and C are incorrect). Carbamazepine is an enzyme inducer, thus decreasing warfarin concentrations (Answer B is incorrect). The patient is indicated for anticoagulation and has been stable for the past 2 years, so drug interactions should be avoided, if

q: A 74-year-old man with a long-standing history of uncontrolled diabetes and uncontrolled hypertension is referred for discussions about hemodialysis preparation. Laboratory data reveal a stable blood urea nitrogen of 42 mg/dL and serum creatinine of 2.8 mg/dL (glomerular filtration rate 18 mL/minute/1.73 m2). The patient's albumin/creatinine ratio is 399 mcg/mg. According to Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes criteria, which is the best assessment of this patient's chronic kidney disease?
opts: A. G4, A2
B. G4, A3
C. G5, A2
D. G5, A3
exp: Chronic kidney disease is classified based on estimated glomerular filtration rate (GFR) and albuminuria (albumin/creatinine ratio [ACR]). This patient's GFR is categorized as G4, severely decreased, because it falls into the range of 15-29 mL/minute/1.73 m2 (Answers C and D are incorrect). Although the patient is being referred for hemodialysis discussions, no hemodialysis has yet occurred. This patient's albuminuria is categorized as A3 based on his ACR that is greater than 300 mcg/mg (Answer

q: A 74-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital after a fall resulting in a tibia and fibula fracture. Four days into her hospital stay, she begins having a cough with fevers and chills. Her temperature is as high as 101.4 degreesF, and her cough is producing yellowish sputum. She has a history of hypothyroidism, hypertension, chronic kidney disease (on hemodialysis three times weekly), and peptic ulcer disease. Her medications include levothyroxine, amlodipine, pantoprazole, sevelamer, erythropoietin, and ferrous sulfate. She has had no antibiotic therapy for over a year. She is given a diagnosis of pneumonia. The methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus incidence in the hospital is 45%. Which is the best empiric therapy for this patient?
opts: A. Cefepime 500 mg intravenously every 24 hours plus azithromycin 500 mg intravenously daily
B. Piperacillin/tazobactam 2.25 g intravenously every 8 hours plus linezolid 600 mg intravenously every 12 hours

C. Meropenem 500 mg intravenously every 24 hours plus tobramycin dosed after each hemodialysis session plus vancomycin 2000 mg intravenously once and then dosed after hemodialysis on the basis of serum concentrations
D. Levofloxacin 750 mg intravenously once; then 500 mg every 48 hours after hemodialysis
exp: Because the patient's illness was diagnosed 4 days into her hospitalization, this is a hospital-acquired pneumonia. Because the methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) incidence is over 20% in the institution, an agent effective against MRSA should be included in the regimen. Therefore, levofloxacin alone and cefepime plus azithromycin are not appropriate (Answers A and D are incorrect). Because the patient has had no intravenous antibiotic exposure in the last 90 days, as well as not

q: C.W. is a 66-year-old man (height 173 cm, weight 145 kg) hospitalized in the post-anesthesia care unit after a laparoscopic gastric sleeve procedure that lasted 2 hours, and bed rest in the hospital is planned for the next 48 hours. His calculated Caprini score is 5. His medical history is significant for hypertension, dyslipidemia, and type 2 diabetes. His medications include lisinopril 20 mg orally daily, rosuvastatin 10 mg orally daily, insulin glargine 50 units subcutaneously daily, and insulin aspart 10 units subcutaneously three times daily before meals. His SCr is 1.1 mg/dL (CrCl 93 mL/min), and all other laboratory values are stable and within normal limits. Which is the optimal regimen for VTE prophylaxis for C.W.?
opts: A. Intermittent pneumatic compressions only
B. Enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously every 12 hours
C. Dalteparin 5000 units subcutaneously every 24 hours
D. Fondaparinux 2.5 mg subcutaneously every 24 hours
exp: Given the patient's Caprini VTE score of 5, he is indicated for anticoagulant therapy with a parenteral anticoagulant to reduce the risk of VTE (Answer A is incorrect). Evidence suggests higher subcutaneous doses of fondaparinux and dalteparin for individuals with BMIs greater than 40 kg/m2 and 50 kg/m2, respectively (Answers C and D are incorrect). Enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously every 12 hours is preferred in patients undergoing bariatric surgery with a BMI greater than 40 kg/m2 (Answer B is c

q: A 30-year-old pregnant woman, at 28 weeks of gestation, presents to your clinic for a routine prenatal checkup. She is healthy, with no significant medical history. Her obstetrician has discussed the option of administering the respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) vaccine to help protect her infant from severe RSV disease after birth. The patient is asking you about the appropriate timing and benefits of the vaccine for her pregnancy. Which is the most appropriate recommendation regarding the RSV vaccine for this patient?
opts: A. The Abrysvo RSV vaccine can be administered now
B. The Arexvy RSV vaccine can be administered now
C. Administer the Abrysvo RSV vaccine at 32 weeks of gestation
D. Advise the patient that she is not a candidate for the RSV vaccine and that her infant should receive nirsevimab
exp: The Abrysvo RSV vaccine is recommended for pregnant women to provide passive immunity to the fetus, protecting the newborn from severe RSV infection during the first few months of life. The ideal window for maternal vaccination is between 32 and 36 weeks of gestation. This timing allows for optimal transfer of protective antibodies to the fetus (Answer A is incorrect; Answer C is correct). Arexvy is not approved in pregnancy (Answer B is incorrect). Nirsevimab is recommended in infants whose mot

q: Differences in CYP450 inhibition may exist within the same class of therapeutic agents. Which group is most accurately matched with the drug in that group with the least CYP450 inhibition?
opts: A. Macrolides - clarithromycin
B. Histamine-2 blockers - cimetidine
C. Fluoroquinolones - ciprofloxacin
D. Calcium channel blockers - amlodipine
exp: Clarithromycin has significant CYP450 inhibition. The correct drug would be azithromycin (Answer A is incorrect). Cimetidine has significant CYP450 inhibition. The correct drug would be famotidine or ranitidine (Answer B is incorrect). Ciprofloxacin has significant CYP450 inhibition. The correct drug would be levofloxacin or moxifloxacin (Answer C is incorrect). The correct answer is amlodipine, which does not inhibit CYP450, like diltiazem or verapamil (Answer D is correct).

q: A 42-year-old man is currently being treated for aggressive diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. At his follow-up clinic visit today after cycle 3, febrile neutropenia is diagnosed. His absolute neutrophil count (ANC) is currently 350 cells/mm3. The patient's vital signs are temperature 38.9 degreesC, heart rate 103 beats/minute, and blood pressure 88/70 mm Hg; his SCr is 1.3 mg/dL and he appears dehydrated. His Multinational Association of Supportive Care in Cancer (MASCC) risk score is calculated to be 16. Which of the following treatment courses is most appropriate at this time, given the patient's presentation?
opts: A. Initiate ciprofloxacin 750 mg orally twice daily and amoxicillin/clavulanate 875 mg orally twice daily in the outpatient setting for low-risk febrile neutropenia

B. Admit the patient to the hospital; initiate cefepime 1 g every 8 hours intravenously plus voriconazole 6 mg/kg intravenously every 12 hours for two doses, followed by 4 mg/kg intravenously every 12 hours for high-risk febrile neutropenia

C. Admit the patient to the hospital; initiate vancomycin 2 g for one dose followed by 1 g intravenously every 12 hours in combination with piperacillin/tazobactam 3.75 g every 8 hours administered over 4 hours intravenously for high-risk febrile neutropenia
D. Admit the patient to the hospital; initiate piperacillin/tazobactam 3.75 g every 8 hours administered over 4 hours intravenously for high-risk febrile neutropenia
exp: Evaluating and stratifying the risk of febrile neutropenia in patients with cancer receiving chemotherapy is crucial to provide appropriate care. This patient is currently experiencing febrile neutropenia with an ANC less than 500 cells/mm3 and a temperature of 38.9 degreesC. Given the patient's symptoms of hypotension, tachycardia, and dehydration and his MASCC score of 16, he has high-risk febrile neutropenia with a potential for poor outcomes (MASCC score less than 21 results in high risk); therefor

q: A 25-year-old (BMI 17 kg/m2) with a history of recurrent major depressive disorder and insomnia has been admitted to the inpatient psychiatric unit after being treated for an overdose. They stopped taking bupropion 1 month ago because it caused weight loss and worsened anxiety. Previous antidepressant experience included anorgasmia while taking fluoxetine. Which medication would be most appropriate for this patient?
opts: A. Desipramine
B. Mirtazapine
C. Paroxetine
D. Vilazodone
exp: The antidepressant prescribed for this patient should be relatively safe in overdose. Tricyclic antidepressants such as desipramine can be fatal if too much is taken. Adverse effects include torsades de pointes and seizures, and overdoses are often fatal (Answer A is incorrect). Paroxetine, though safer in overdose, is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor and might cause anorgasmia similar to what the patient experienced with fluoxetine (Answer C is incorrect). Mirtazapine is associated with

q: A 55-year-old man with a history of type 2 diabetes and hypertension is admitted to the ICU with septic shock secondary to community-acquired pneumonia. He is sedated, intubated, and receiving mechanical ventilation. After initial fluid resuscitation, his mean arterial pressure remains borderline at 65 mm Hg while receiving a norepinephrine infusion. Which assessment strategy is most appropriate to evaluate fluid responsiveness?
opts: A. Evaluate his current central venous pressure (CVP) through his central line
B. Assess stroke volume variation (SVV) using arterial waveform analysis
C. Measure pulmonary capillary wedge pressure through a pulmonary artery catheter
D. Obtain a transthoracic echocardiogram to assess left ventricular chamber size
exp: Dynamic parameters such as SVV are superior to static measurements in predicting fluid responsiveness in patients with septic shock receiving controlled mechanical ventilation (Answer B is correct). Answer A is incorrect because a single static measurement of CVP (eg, 8 mm Hg) does not reliably predict fluid responsiveness and fails to account for the dynamic changes in preload. Answer C is not recommended because of its invasive nature and limited usefulness in this scenario compared with dynam

q: A 28-year-old woman is recently diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome. The condition has limited her ability to conceive a child, and she wants to become pregnant. Which agent is the most appropriate choice to improve fertility?
opts: A. Canagliflozin
B. Metformin
C. Letrozole
D. Estrogen/progesterone
exp: Oral agents that improve fertility in patients with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) include clomiphene and letrozole (Answer C is correct). Metformin may help with unwanted symptoms associated with PCOS, such as excessive hair growth, but is not recommended to improve fertility (Answer B is incorrect). Sodium-glucose transport protein 2 inhibitors have no use in PCOS (Answer A is incorrect). Estrogen/progesterone is used to improve unwanted symptoms of PCOS in those who do not want to become pr

q: Which best describes the evidence-based approach to antithrombotic therapy in a patient with nonvalvular atrial fibrillation (NVAF) undergoing percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI)?
opts: A. Triple therapy with warfarin, aspirin, and clopidogrel has shown reduction in the risk of any bleeding similar to dual therapy with warfarin and clopidogrel
B. Triple therapy with warfarin, aspirin, and clopidogrel has shown superiority to warfarin and clopidogrel for major cardiac events

C. All direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) in combination with a P2Y12 inhibitor significantly reduced major bleeding compared with warfarin triple therapy in randomized trials comparing dual and triple therapy for NVAF and PCI
D. Clopidogrel is the preferred P2Y12 inhibitor because it was the predominant P2Y12 inhibitor used in all randomized trials comparing dual and triple therapy for NVAF and PCI
exp: The WOEST trial showed a reduction in any bleeding and major cardiac events with warfarin and clopidogrel (Answers A and B are incorrect). Although DOACs are preferred to warfarin, edoxaban combined with clopidogrel did not meet statistical significance in its primary outcome of major or clinically relevant bleeding compared with warfarin triple therapy (Answer C is incorrect). Less than 15% of the study population in each randomized trial comparing dual and triple antithrombotic therapy receive

q: A 56-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with concerns of feeling unwell and a persistent headache. His spouse states the patient has been experiencing headaches for the past 2 days and has been drinking a new "colored liquid" that he placed in a Mason jar in the refrigerator. The patient's medical history is significant for an alcohol use disorder. He is minimally responsive but is maintaining his airway, and his oxygen saturation is 100%. His vital signs are as follows: blood pressure 98/61 mm Hg, heart rate 105 beats/minute, and respiratory rate 18 breaths/minute. Available laboratory values include Na 143 mEq/L, K 3.6 mEq/L, Cl 103 mEq/L, serum urea nitrogen 10 mg/dL, SCr 2.1 mg/dL, glucose 299 mg/dL, lactate 1.2 mmol/L, ethanol 36 mg/dL, and serum osmolality 412 mOsm/kg. Intravenous access is obtained, and the venous blood gas results are as follows: pH 7.0, Pco2 67.9, and HCO3 6.9 mEq/L. Unfortunately, methanol and ethylene glycol concentrations are only tested once a day in the morning at your institution. Which intervention is most appropriate for this patient?
opts: A. Initiate renal replacement therapy
B. Administer fomepizole 10 mg/kg orally
C. Administer 50 g of activated charcoal orally
D. Administer fomepizole 15 mg/kg intravenously
exp: This patient is presenting with an anion gap (33.1 mEq/L), acidosis (pH 7.0), and an osmolality gap of 96.1 mOsm/kg. Although actual methanol and ethylene glycol concentrations are unavailable, these data combined with the suggestive patient presentation should lead to the initiation of fomepizole therapy (Answer D is correct). Oral administration of fomepizole can be effective; however, this is only a consideration if intravenous access cannot be obtained, which is not the case here (Answer B i

q: A 47-year-old woman with a 12-year history of type 2 diabetes is currently receiving metformin 1000 mg twice daily, insulin glargine 22 units once daily, lisinopril 40 mg once daily, and atorvastatin 40 mg once daily. Her A1c today is 7.9% (personal goal less than 7%). Her blood pressure is well controlled with this regimen. Her renal function has been declining over the last 6-12 months, and her urine albumin/creatinine has been increasing over the same period (currently 52 mL/minute/1.73 m2 and 932 mg/g respectively). Which option may have the most benefit to improve the patient's glycemic control while also helping to reduce the progression of her diabetic kidney disease?
opts: A. Semaglutide
B. Alogliptin
C. Insulin aspart
D. Dapagliflozin
exp: The standard of care for patients with type 2 diabetes (T2D) and renal insufficiency has historically been optimizing glycemic and blood pressure control, the latter being treated with an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker. Current guidelines now also recommend the addition of a sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitor (SGLT2i) and/or nonsteroidal aldosterone antagonist because both have been shown to reduce the progress of kidney disease in patients with T2D

q: E.F., a 79-year-old man, was admitted to a long-term care facility after several falls at home. His medical history is significant for hypertension, osteoarthritis of the knee, Alzheimer disease (AD) with hallucinations that are not bothersome, and newly diagnosed overactive bladder (OAB). He takes metoprolol succinate 100 mg daily and donepezil 10 mg daily. His blood pressure is 116/74 mm Hg and pulse rate is 66 beats/min. Basic metabolic panel results are all within reference range; 25-hydroxyvitamin D concentration is 50 ng/mL and thyrotropin is 1.49 mU/L. His Mini-Mental State Examination score is 16/30. As the consultant pharmacist reviewing his medications, which is most appropriate to initiate for E.F. at this time?
opts: A. Oxybutynin
B. Solifenacin
C. Quetiapine
D. Mirabegron
exp: Given the patient's history of AD, mirabegron is the preferred agent to treat OAB because of the minimal risk of anticholinergic effects unlike oxybutynin and solifenacin (Answers A and B are incorrect). Despite a history of hypertension, the patient's disease has been well controlled, making mirabegron appropriate (Answer D is correct). Quetiapine would be reasonable for hallucinations; however, because this patient is not bothered by them, adding antipsychotics at this time would not be approp

q: J.N. is a 54-year-old man (height 177.8 cm, weight 81 kg) with a history of hypertension who was recently diagnosed with chronic kidney disease (CKD) 3b, A2 (glomerular filtration rate [GFR], 40 mL/min/1.73 m2). J.N.'s medical history is significant for hypertension, and he currently takes losartan 100 mg orally daily. His blood pressure in the clinic today was 146/82 mm Hg. His most recent laboratory test results show a K concentration of 4.5 mEq/L. Which evidence-based lifestyle intervention would be most appropriate to counsel J.N. on today?
opts: A. Limit sodium to less than 2 g/d
B. Restrict protein to 0.4 g/kg/d
C. Encourage weight loss of 10% body mass
D. Increase physical activity to 150 min/wk
exp: The 2024 Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) guidelines on the evaluation and management of CKD recommend a variety of lifestyle interventions to delay the progression of CKD and reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease, including increasing physical activity and encouraging weight loss. However, J.N. is only slightly overweight, and such recommendations may not be the most relevant at this time (Answers C and D are incorrect). Patients with CKD are recommended to target a goal pr

q: In a patient initiated on carbamazepine, the drug concentration is 5 mg/L at 1 week after initiation. Which is the best assessment of this concentration?
opts: A. The concentration is below the therapeutic range, and the current dose should be maintained because the concentration will increase over the next few weeks
B. The concentration is in the therapeutic range, and the current dose must be increased because the concentration will decrease over the next few weeks
C. The concentration is above the therapeutic range, and the current dose must be decreased to avoid toxicity
D. The concentration is in the therapeutic range, and the current dose should be maintained long term
exp: Carbamazepine is an autoinducer. Therefore, over the first few weeks of therapy, carbamazepine clearance will increase. For this reason, carbamazepine doses are usually initiated at 50% of the ultimate goal dose and increased over 2-3 weeks. In this case, the concentration is in the lower end of the therapeutic range (4-12 mg/L) (Answers A and C are incorrect). However, because the patient is only in the first week of therapy, the concentration is expected to decrease over the next few weeks. At

q: A 29-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider with signs and symptoms consistent with gonococcal urethritis. During this visit, the provider mentions HIV preexposure prophylaxis (PrEP) to gauge her interest. The patient is agreeable to trying PrEP and verbalizes understanding of the medication as an HIV preventive only. She is prescribed tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine daily. After taking her first dose, the patient asks whether she is "protected" from acquiring HIV through receptive vaginal sex. Which is the best answer to her question?
opts: A. Yes, but she would need to take a "double dose" today
B. Yes, but she should continue using a barrier method of contraception
C. No, adequate concentrations are not reached until 21 days of daily use
D. No, adequate concentrations are not reached until 7 days of daily use
exp: Patients receiving oral PrEP are unlikely to achieve adequate protection until cervicovaginal concentrations are appreciable (after 21 days of daily use) (Answer C is correct). Taking a double dose resembles the "on-demand" dosing strategy. This answer is incomplete from a dosing scheme perspective; moreover, this has not been studied in women (Answer A is incorrect). Although she should continue using barrier methods to protect against sexually transmitted infections, she is not considered to b

q: Z.P. was diagnosed with peripheral neuropathy 4 years ago. They have been unsuccessfully treated with multiple topical agents. They are currently treated with gabapentin at the maximum therapeutic dose and are reporting continued neuropathic pain symptoms. Vitals in clinic today include blood pressure 126/74 mm Hg and heart rate 72 beats/minute. Laboratory values include HLA-B*1502 allele confirmed. Which prescription treatment option would be the best for Z.P.?
opts: A. Carbamazepine
B. Salicylate
C. Tramadol
D. Venlafaxine
exp: The patient has failed multiple topical agents, and the question is asking specifically for a prescription product; salicylate is topical and over-the-counter (Answer B is incorrect). The detection of HLA-B*1502 allele eliminates carbamazepine because of the increased risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome (Answer A is incorrect). Tramadol is a second-line option and would not be the best choice (Answer C is incorrect) because there is a first-line treatment option in venlafaxine (Answer D is correct)

q: The daughter of an 85-year-old man with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and essential tremor is interested in changing his inhaler to a device that facilitates adherence and ease of use and does not require forceful inhalation because of his low inspiratory flow rate. The following delivery devices are determined to be compatible with his current treatment regimen. Which device is best to recommend?
opts: A. Ellipta
B. HandiHaler
C. Hydrofluoroalkane (HFA)
D. Pressair
exp: Ellipta requires a deep and slow inhalation that is easier to use than other dry powder inhalers (DPIs) that require more forceful inhalation (Answer A is correct). Although inhalation with the HandiHaler is easier, it is not ideal as far as ease of use because it requires loading and piercing of the capsule before administration of each dose (Answer B is incorrect). HFA inhalers require coordination, which often results in difficulty with technique (Answer C is incorrect). Pressair is a DPI tha

q: A unit-based clinical generalist pharmacist is evaluating an inpatient who is being treated for emergent hypotension in the operating room. The patient is a 79-year-old man who was initially admitted for treatment of osteomyelitis. The pharmacist was requested to prepare push-dose norepinephrine for this patient and wants to verify the correct dose. Using the Population/Problem/Patient, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome (PICO) approach, which is the most relevant P term to consider in framing this clinical question?
opts: A. Osteomyelitis
B. Geriatric
C. Hypotension
D. Male
exp: Although all four of these options could be considered P terms, the most relevant P term is hypotension because it most directly addresses the use of the intervention being considered (push-dose norepinephrine) (Answer C is correct). Osteomyelitis is also relevant to the case as the reason for admission; however, if incorporated into a search, this variable is less essential to the root question and could prevent relevant results from appearing; for example, using "AND" in combination with other

q: A 71-year-old woman (height 172.72 cm, weight 64 kg) with a history of osteoarthritis (OA), type 2 diabetes (hemoglobin A1c 6.2% two months ago), and hypertension presents for an annual wellness visit. Current medications include lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, rosuvastatin 10 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily, and OTC naproxen 220 mg 1 or 2 tablets one to four times daily as needed for OA pain. Vital signs are blood pressure 124/74 mm Hg, pulse rate 80 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and temperature 37 degreesC. Laboratory values include K 4.1 mEq/L, SCr 0.8 mg/dL (baseline), estimated glomerular filtration rate 65 mL/min/1.73 m2, fasting blood glucose 98 mg/dL, total cholesterol 150 mg/dL, low-density lipoprotein cholesterol 73 mg/dL, high-density lipoprotein cholesterol 48 mg/dL, and triglycerides 144 mg/dL. Her 10-year cardiovascular disease risk is 23%. Which is most appropriate to recommend?
opts: A. Discontinue aspirin 81 mg daily
B. Discontinue naproxen 220 mg as needed
C. Increase lisinopril to 40 mg daily
D. Decrease metformin to 500 mg twice daily
exp: The patient's high 10-year cardiovascular disease risk, type 2 diabetes, and hypertension previously gave her an indication for low-dose aspirin therapy for primary prevention. However, now that she is older than 60 years according to US Preventive Services Task Force and 70 years according to the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association (ACC/AHA) guidelines, the risk outweighs the benefit of aspirin therapy (Answer A is correct). The patient's renal function is stable, and she

q: A 37-year-old man (height 172 cm, weight 88 kg) is intubated for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure secondary to chronic obstructive pulmonary disease exacerbation. Current medications include fentanyl 100 mcg/hour continuous infusion, dexmedetomidine 0.8 mcg/kg/hour continuous infusion, nebulized albuterol/ipratropium every 6 hours, and prednisone 40 mg per feeding tube once daily. Vital signs are as follows: blood pressure 138/90 mm Hg, heart rate 68 beats/minute, respiratory rate 20 breaths/minute, afebrile. The patient's current Critical-Care Pain Observation Tool score is 1 and Richmond Agitation-Sedation Score is +1. Which strategy is most appropriate to optimize the patient's pain and sedation needs?
opts: A. Increase fentanyl infusion rate from 100 mcg/hour to 125 mcg/hour
B. Discontinue dexmedetomidine and initiate propofol 10 mcg/kg/min continuous infusion
C. Administer a one-time fentanyl 50 mcg intravenous bolus and reassess in 1 hour
D. Increase dexmedetomidine infusion rate from 0.8 mcg/kg/hour to 0.9 mcg/kg/hour
exp: Clinical assessments show that the patient is currently not in pain (Critical-Care Pain Observation Tool [CPOT] score is less than 3) but has uncontrolled agitation (Richmond Agitation-Sedation Score [RASS] greater than 0). Current PADIS guidelines recommend light sedation (defined as RASS 0 to −2) for most critically ill patients. Given that the patient is not in worsening pain, there is no need to change the current analgesic therapy, and the fentanyl can remain at 100 mcg/hour. Dexmedetomidin

q: S.D. is a 73-year-old female patient presenting to the ED with left-sided weakness, dysphasia, and left-sided facial numbness. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Her current symptoms began 5½ hours ago. A random blood glucose reading is 54 mg/dL; her current blood pressure is 144/76 mm Hg. S.D. is diagnosed with a major ischemic stroke. Which is the best treatment recommendation for S.D.?
opts: A. Apixaban
B. Aspirin and clopidogrel
C. Aspirin and dipyridamole
D. Alteplase
exp: Because the patient presents after the 4½-hour window for using alteplase, alteplase is not an option (Answer D is incorrect). Initiating dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) with aspirin and clopidogrel for 90 days after a major stroke would be best (Answer B is correct). Apixaban is indicated for secondary stroke prophylaxis in patients with atrial fibrillation, but this patient does not have atrial fibrillation (Answer A is incorrect). Aspirin and dipyridamole may be an option after the 90-day co

q: A 66-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital's general medicine unit with concerns of fatigue, malaise, constipation, and some mild confusion. Her other medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and gastroesophageal reflux disease. Home medications include lisinopril 40 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, vitamin D 2000 units daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily, and pantoprazole 40 mg daily. Laboratory values include Na 135 mEq/L, K 3.9 mEq/L, SCr 1.2 mg/dL (baseline), glucose 140 mg/dL, calcium 11.2 g/dL, and albumin 4.0 g/dL. The medical team is considering administering zoledronic acid for hypercalcemia of malignancy (HOM). Which is most accurate regarding the patient's medication regimen and hypercalcemia?
opts: A. Vitamin D administration will keep serum calcium concentrations lower over the long term
B. Hydrochlorothiazide administration will help lower serum calcium concentrations
C. Serum calcium concentrations will begin to decrease within 2 to 3 days of zoledronic acid administration
D. Pamidronate is more efficacious in hypercalcemia of malignancy than zoledronic acid
exp: Intravenous bisphosphonates are recommended first line in HOM because of their efficacy in reducing serum calcium concentrations, which takes 2 to 3 days for onset of action and 5 to 7 days for full laboratory effects (Answer C is correct). Zoledronic acid is often preferred to pamidronate because of consistent serum calcium-lowering effects in clinical trials (Answer D is incorrect). Vitamin D administration increases gut absorption of calcium, which would keep serum calcium concentrations high

q: A patient with hypertension and G4, A3 chronic kidney disease (CKD) is newly diagnosed with type 2 diabetes (A1c 7.1%). She has a stable, but slowly declining glomerular filtration rate (GFR) of 24 mL/min/1.73 m2 and is currently receiving lisinopril 20 mg orally daily (maximum dose tolerated by patient). Which is most appropriate to initiate for this patient to delay progression of CKD?
opts: A. Metformin
B. Metformin plus dapagliflozin
C. Irbesartan
D. Dapagliflozin
exp: The combination of an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor and an angiotensin II receptor blocker is not likely to provide greater benefit and is likely to increase adverse reactions (Answer C is incorrect). This patient has a GFR of less than 30 mL/min, indicating that metformin should not be initiated (Answers A and B are incorrect). According to the Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes 2024 guidelines, first-line therapy for diabetes and CKD should include renin-angiotensin system inh

q: A 21-year-old woman presents to the clinic with genital itching and vesicles on her vulva. She is sexually active with one partner who has a history of herpes. Her partner does not always use a condom when they have sex. She was appropriately treated for her initial herpes simplex infection 10 months ago. This is her seventh recurrence since initial diagnosis. Which therapy is best to recommend?
opts: A. Valacyclovir 500 mg orally twice daily to be used for 5 days whenever she notices a recurrence beginning
B. Acyclovir 400 mg orally three times daily to be used for 10 days whenever she notices a recurrence beginning
C. Suppressive therapy with famciclovir 250 mg orally three times daily
D. Suppressive therapy with valacyclovir 500 mg orally daily
exp: Patient-initiated therapy is important for people with occasional recurrences (fewer than six per year) of herpes simplex virus infection because recurrent infections resolve more rapidly than the initial infection. Antiviral agents should be initiated as soon as possible. Because the patient is having several recurrences (six or more a year), patient-initiated therapy is insufficient (Answers A and B are incorrect). Suppressive therapy is given twice daily or once daily, not three times daily;

q: A 21-year-old with new-onset schizophrenia presents to your office for a medication check. They were hospitalized for a psychotic episode 6 months ago, for which they received quetiapine. They currently take quetiapine 600 mg at bedtime. They have gained 11.34 kg (current weight 86.18 kg, current BMI 32.6 kg/m2). They are alert and oriented × 3, neatly groomed, and dressed appropriately. They continue to hear voices that make negative comments about them. The voices still make them feel sad, but they no longer feel compelled to do anything about it. They believe the voices are real. They remain socially withdrawn and live at home with their parents. Which is the best recommendation?
opts: A. Continue quetiapine at current dose
