B. Change quetiapine to cariprazine
C. Change quetiapine to clozapine
D. Change quetiapine to olanzapine
exp: This patient's response to quetiapine has plateaued, and it is the first agent this patient has tried for schizophrenia. This patient has also gained 13% of their body weight on quetiapine. The FDA recommends considering a change in antipsychotic if a patient gains 7% or more of their body weight while taking an antipsychotic. Therefore, the risks of continuing this medication outweigh the benefits (Answer A is incorrect). Olanzapine, together with clozapine, is associated with the greatest degr

q: A 49-year-old man presents to the primary care clinic with concerns of ongoing knee pain for the past 8 months. Radiography of the right knee reveals no degenerative changes. No traumatic events are reported, and physical examination findings are negative for injuries. His medical history is significant for recurrent bouts of gastroesophageal reflux and hypertension. Which is best to recommend for this patient?
opts: A. Topical capsaicin
B. Oral naproxen
C. Topical diclofenac
D. Oral celecoxib
exp: Although there are no pertinent radiographic findings, the patient's expression of pain should be believed. Radiographic findings, or the lack thereof, are not predictive of pain; therefore, he should be treated. Topical capsaicin has a conditional, not strong, recommendation for use, according to the treatment guidelines (Answer A is incorrect). The patient has a history of gastroesophageal reflux and hypertension. Although not of concern from a mortality/serious adverse effect perspective, the

q: A 34-year-old woman is 8 weeks into a third pregnancy. The patient has had significant nausea and vomiting throughout the first trimester of each of the prior pregnancies. She is currently experiencing nausea and vomiting that has not improved with nonpharmacologic options, such as the use of ginger products and dietary changes. Which recommendation is most appropriate at this time?
opts: A. Continue nonpharmacologic options
B. Initiate pyridoxine
C. Initiate doxylamine
D. Initiate ondansetron
exp: Because this patient's symptoms have not resolved with current nonpharmacologic approaches, pharmacologic treatment is warranted (Answer A is incorrect). Pyridoxine is an appropriate first-line therapy to treat nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and can be used with or without doxylamine (Answer B is correct). Doxylamine should not be used as monotherapy for nausea and vomiting of pregnancy (Answer C is incorrect). Ondansetron should only be considered if nausea and vomiting are not controlled wit

q: A 34-year-old presents to your outpatient clinic to discuss smoking cessation. This patient is being considered for bariatric surgery and needs to be off all nicotine products for at least 3 months before surgery. The patient has a 20 pack-year history of smoking cigarettes (smokes 1 pack/day, first cigarette within 30 minutes of awakening). They have previously tried to quit "cold turkey." This patient is almost through their workup for surgery and does not want to wait longer than needed. Which treatment would be best?
opts: A. Bupropion
B. Nicotine gum
C. Nicotine patch
D. Varenicline
exp: Nicotine replacement products that are used as needed, such as nicotine gum, are not as successful at helping patients quit smoking (Answer B is incorrect). This patient needs to be off nicotine products for at least 3 months before surgery. Using a modality such as the nicotine patch may delay the patient's surgery date, because they have to wean off cigarettes and then wean off the patch (Answer C is incorrect). According to the ATS clinical guidelines, varenicline is recommended over both bup

q: A 36-year-old is currently 34 weeks gestation with her first pregnancy. She has no significant past medical history. Her blood pressure at her appointment last week was 146/94 mm Hg, and at today's appointment is 152/96 mm Hg. Her urine dipstick at today's appointment shows 2+ protein. All other lab parameters are within normal limits. What treatment is best to recommend?
opts: A. Labor induction for immediate delivery
B. Start nifedipine extended-release 30 mg orally daily
C. Start magnesium sulfate 1 g intravenously every 8 hours
D. Start labetalol 100 mg intravenously every 12 hours
exp: This patient is experiencing pregnancy-induced hypertension because she is currently further along than 20 weeks gestation and does not have a pre-existing history of hypertension. She is not experiencing any urgent warning signs and has not progressed to a diagnosis of pre-eclampsia; therefore, immediate delivery is not necessary because she is not at full term (Answer A is incorrect). The use of oral nifedipine is a safe and appropriate choice for pregnancy-induced hypertension (Answer B is co

q: A 71-year-old man (height 175 cm, weight 93 kg) has a medical history of atrial fibrillation (AF) (CHA2DS2-VASc score 2) with initial onset 3 years ago and bioprosthetic aortic valve replacement 4 months ago for management of symptomatic aortic regurgitation. He presents to the outpatient cardiology clinic requesting next steps for management of anticoagulation. Other conditions include hypertension and obesity. His medications include warfarin 5 mg orally daily, metoprolol succinate 100 mg orally daily, and losartan 100 mg orally daily. His INR is 2.6, and all other laboratory values are within normal limits. The patient is concerned about long-term warfarin management and is seeking alternatives. Which best describes the evidence-based approach to managing this patient's valvular heart disease with AF?
opts: A. Continue dose-adjusted warfarin to an INR target of 2.5
B. Discontinue warfarin and initiate aspirin 81 mg orally daily
C. Discontinue warfarin and initiate rivaroxaban 20 mg orally daily
D. Discontinue warfarin. No additional antithrombotic therapy is indicated
exp: Patients with a surgical aortic bioprosthetic valve with an indication for anticoagulation because of AF and a CHA2DS2-VASc score of 2 can discontinue warfarin after 3-6 months and start guideline-directed anticoagulation such as rivaroxaban 20 mg orally daily (Answer C is correct). Although warfarin may be a reasonable anticoagulant for the prevention of stroke in a patient with AF, a higher INR target (3.0) would be indicated for this patient with a bioprosthetic aortic valve and an additional

q: A patient with diabetes and G3b, A3 chronic kidney disease is currently taking lisinopril. She has a blood pressure of 118/78 mm Hg and presents complaining of a dry cough. She has the following stable laboratory values after trying appropriate diet modifications for the past 3 months: serum creatinine 2.7 mg/dL, serum potassium 4.6 mEq/L, serum calcium 9.5 mg/dL, serum phosphate 5.8 mg/dL, serum albumin 2.5 g/dL, serum parathyroid hormone 301 pg/mL. Which medications would be most appropriate to initiate at this time?
opts: A. Discontinue lisinopril, initiate losartan and calcium acetate
B. Continue lisinopril, initiate sevelamer carbonate
C. Continue lisinopril, initiate calcium acetate
D. Discontinue lisinopril, initiate losartan and sevelamer carbonate
exp: This patient has diabetes and chronic kidney disease (CKD) category G3b, A3 along with hyperphosphatemia and hyperparathyroidism. A corrected calcium calculation also reveals the presence of hypercalcemia [measured Ca + (0.8)(4 - serum albumin) = 9.5 mg/dL + (0.8)(4 - 2.5) = 9.5 + 1.2 = 10.7 mg/dL]. Because of the continually elevated phosphate level, initiation of a phosphate binder is appropriate. With the corrected calcium elevated, choosing a calcium-containing phosphate binder places the pa

q: A 59-year-old man with New York Heart Association class III heart failure with reduced ejection fraction presented to the ED yesterday with a 13.6-kg weight gain over the past month, bilateral lower extremity edema, and worsening shortness of breath on exertion. His home dose of furosemide is 80 mg orally twice daily. Pertinent laboratory values include Na 136 mEq/L, K 4.8 mEq/L, serum urea nitrogen 20 mg/dL, SCr 2.0 mg/dL (baseline 1.3 mg/dL), and N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide 1540 pg/mL. Today, his laboratory values remain unchanged. His blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg and pulse rate is 90 beats/min. His oxygen saturation is 89% on 6-L nasal cannula. The patient was initiated on furosemide 40 mg intravenously twice daily with an inadequate response to diuresis and is now experiencing shortness of breath at rest. Which next step is most appropriate to promote diuresis?
opts: A. Add hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg orally daily
B. Increase furosemide to 80 mg intravenously twice daily
C. Administer milrinone 0.125-mcg/kg/h continuous infusion
D. Administer intravenous nitroglycerin 5-mcgg/min continuous infusion
exp: After an ineffective initial attempt at diuresis, this patient requires escalated diuretic strategies to address his volume overload. Guidelines recommend initial intravenous loop diuretic 1 to 2.5 times the patient's home diuretic dose either as intermittent bolus or by continuous infusion, according to the DOSE trial (Answer B is correct). Optimization of high-dose intravenous loop diuretics is recommended before adding a thiazide or thiazide-like diuretic because of the increased risk of impa

q: A 47-year-old woman with a 7-year history of type 2 diabetes and a 3-year history of diabetic kidney disease is receiving insulin glargine 36 units once daily. She weighs 74 kg. Last week, her A1c was 7.6% (personal goal less than 7%). Her average morning, fasting glucose is 202 mg/dl. Her average postprandial glucose after breakfast, lunch, and dinner is 121 mg/dL, 110 mg/dL, and 130 mg/dL, respectively. Her most recent serum creatinine was 1.9 mg/dL (estimated glomerular filtration rate 24 mL/minute/1.73 m2). Which is best option to improve this patient's glycemic control?
opts: A. Increase insulin glargine
B. Add insulin aspart at breakfast
C. Add metformin
D. Add dapagliflozin
exp: This patient's postprandial glucose averages are good, whereas her fasting glucose average is very poor. Basal insulins, such as insulin glargine, help improve morning, fasting glucose concentrations, making it the correct answer (Answer A is correct). Adding a rapid-acting insulin is incorrect because it will not improve the patient's fasting blood glucose levels and may lead to postprandial hypoglycemia caused by already tight postprandial control (Answer B is incorrect). Metformin is contrain

q: A 10-year-old boy with severe eosinophilic asthma is determined to be a candidate for add-on biologic therapy because of persisting symptoms with multiple exacerbations in the past year despite optimized therapy. The patient has no contraindications to biologic therapies with a baseline blood eosinophil count of 175 cells/mm3. Which biologic treatment is best to recommend?
opts: A. Intravenous reslizumab
B. Subcutaneous benralizumab
C. Subcutaneous mepolizumab
D. Subcutaneous omalizumab
exp: Mepolizumab is best because it is indicated for add-on maintenance therapy in severe asthma in patients 6 years and older with eosinophilic asthma (Answer C is correct). Reslizumab and benralizumab are incorrect; although both are indicated for eosinophilic asthma, they are not appropriate because of the patient's age. Reslizumab is indicated for patients 18 years and older, and benralizumab is indicated for patients 12 years and older (Answers A and B are incorrect). Omalizumab is an anti-immun

q: A 70-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease and dysphagia requires medication administration by a G-tube. His current medication regimen includes metoprolol succinate 50 mg extended release once daily, clopidogrel 75 mg once daily, omeprazole 40-mg delayed-release capsule once daily, and duloxetine 30-mg delayed-release capsule once daily. Which best depicts the medication that can be safely crushed and administered by the G-tube?
opts: A. Metoprolol succinate
B. Clopidogrel
C. Omeprazole
D. Duloxetine
exp: Clopidogrel is a tablet that can be crushed without affecting its efficacy (Answer B is correct). Metoprolol succinate is extended release and should not be crushed (Answer A is incorrect). Duloxetine and pantoprazole are both delayed-release capsules, so they should not be crushed but may be opened to be administered by G-tube (Answers C and D are incorrect).

q: A 38-year-old patient presents to the hospital with pain and swelling in the left lower extremity. Physical examination reveals diffuse erythema and swelling appearing to originate at the left ankle. There is no obvious purulent drainage, but some edema is present. The patient reports difficulty breathing when taking amoxicillin/clavulanate about 5 years ago. Which is the best empiric treatment for this patient?
opts: A. Aztreonam 1 g intravenously every 8 hours
B. Cefazolin 2 g intravenously every 8 hours
C. Ciprofloxacin 400 mg intravenously every 12 hours
D. Vancomycin 1000 mg intravenously every 12 hours
exp: This patient likely has cellulitis attributed to Streptococcus, given the lack of purulence and poorly demarcated presentation. Aztreonam lacks activity against gram-positive organisms, making it a poor empiric choice for most cases of cellulitis (Answer A is incorrect). The unique side chain of cefazolin allows it to be safely used when patients have a reported penicillin allergy (Answer B is correct). Ciprofloxacin does not have reliable activity against gram-positive organisms, even when it i

q: A 42-year-old patient reports headaches occurring 5 or 6 times per month that interfere with her ability to work. The episodes last 6 to 10 hours and are described as unilateral head pain, 8/10 on a 10-point scale. Her medical history includes epilepsy, peripheral vascular disease, and type 2 diabetes. Current medications are carbamazepine 200 mg orally twice daily, cilostazol 100 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg orally daily, and insulin glargine 26 units subcutaneously every evening. Which is the best acute treatment for this patient's headache disorder?
opts: A. Ubrogepant
B. Sumatriptan
C. Lasmiditan
D. Dihydroergotamine
exp: The patient is presenting with migraine headache (severe, given the pain rating 8/10). Triptans and dihydroergotamine should be avoided because of the patient's history of peripheral vascular disease (Answers B and D are incorrect). Ubrogepant should be avoided because of a drug interaction with carbamazepine (avoid use with strong CYP3A4 inducers; Answer A is incorrect). Lasmiditan is the best option for treating this patient's acute migraine headaches (Answer C is correct).

q: C.S. is a 66-year-old woman (height 163 cm, weight 75 kg) with newly diagnosed nonvalvular atrial fibrillation. Her current rate control includes bisoprolol 5 mg orally daily, and she is asymptomatic. Her medical history is otherwise significant for type 2 diabetes, diastolic heart failure (ejection fraction [EF] 55%), and osteoarthritis of the hip. Her medication regimen also consists of spironolactone 25 mg orally daily, metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg orally daily, dulaglutide 3 mg subcutaneously weekly, and meloxicam 15 mg orally daily. Her pulse rate is 72 beats/min and blood pressure is 126/80 mm Hg. Her SCr is 1.0 mg/dL with a CrCl of 55 mL/min. All other laboratory values are within normal limits. She denies use of tobacco or alcohol. Which best describes C.S.'s risk of stroke and major bleeding?
opts: A. High risk of stroke with CHA2DS2-VASc score of 2 and moderate risk of major bleed with HAS-BLED score of 2
B. High risk of stroke with CHA2DS2-VASc score of 3 and high risk of major bleed with HAS-BLED score of 3
C. High risk of stroke with CHA2DS2-VASc score of 4 and moderate risk of major bleed with HAS-BLED score of 2
D. High risk of stroke with CHA2DS2-VASc score of 3 and high risk of major bleed with HAS-BLED score of 2
exp: Given the patient's calculated CHA2DS2-VASc score of 4 (age 65-74 years, type 2 diabetes, heart failure, and female sex) and calculated HAS-BLED score of 2 (age older than 65 and medication use predisposing the patient to bleeding with meloxicam use), she is considered at high risk of stroke and moderate risk of major bleeding (Answer C is correct; Answers A, B, and D are incorrect). Previously, the risk factor of congestive heart failure represented patients with an EF of 40% or less, but recen

q: A 49-year-old man experienced a witnessed in-hospital cardiac arrest with pulseless ventricular tachycardia and achieved return of spontaneous circulation after 10 minutes. The physician selected a 37 degreesC target for targeted temperature management, and the patient will be cooled to target over 24 hours. The patient is mechanically ventilated, and current medications include fentanyl 50 mcg/hour continuous infusion, propofol 15 mcg/kg/minute, vancomycin 1 gram intravenously every 12 hours, piperacillin/tazobactam 3.375 grams intravenously every 8 hours, and heparin 5000 units subcutaneously every 8 hours. Which strategy is best to optimize the patient's clinical outcomes during targeted temperature management?
opts: A. Add rocuronium 1 mg/kg boluses as needed for shivering
B. Change heparin from prophylactic to therapeutic dosing
C. Add acetaminophen 650 mg orally every 4 hours as needed for fevers
D. Change propofol to midazolam infusion
exp: Fever prevention is an essential component of managing patients under targeted temperature management. As-needed acetaminophen is part of institutional anti-shivering protocols for its central mechanism of fever reduction (Answer C is correct). Although shivering prevention is needed to limit development of fevers during hypothermia, paralytic use is not routinely recommended because of safety considerations. Additionally, other medications are likely to achieve shivering prevention in most pati

q: A 22-year-old woman who uses heroin regularly has just discovered that she is pregnant. She has no other significant medical history. She wants to stop using heroin. Which medication would be best?
opts: A. Acamprosate
B. Chlordiazepoxide
C. Methadone
D. Naltrexone
exp: Methadone or buprenorphine is currently considered the standard of care for women who are pregnant who wish to detoxify from opioids (Answer C is correct). There is little research to support the use of naltrexone in women who are pregnant (Answer D is incorrect). Acamprosate is used for patients who have alcohol use disorder, not opioid use disorder (Answer A is incorrect). Chlordiazepoxide is used to treat acute withdrawal from alcohol, but it should not be combined with opioids and should be

q: A woman with a history of persistent asthma presents to the ED because of worsening shortness of breath at rest. She is able to converse with some difficulty. She reports little improvement since she administered 4 puffs of her albuterol metered dose inhaler (MDI) at home 4 hours ago. She has no recent history of oral corticosteroid (OCS) use. Her forced expiratory volume in the first second of expiration (FEV1) is 48% of predicted. Her pulse rate is 102 beats/min. Her oxygen saturation on room air is 90%, and she is administered supplemental oxygen. Which therapy is best to initially treat this patient's asthma exacerbation?
opts: A. Albuterol and ipratropium by nebulizer every 20 minutes for 1 hour plus intravenous corticosteroids
B. Albuterol and ipratropium by nebulizer every 20 minutes for 1 hour plus OCS
C. Albuterol MDI up to 3 doses in the first hour
D. Albuterol MDI up to 3 doses in the first hour plus OCS
exp: The patient's clinical presentation is indicative of a severe asthma exacerbation, based on an FEV1 less than 50% of predicted with dyspnea at rest. She should receive an OCS in addition to albuterol and ipratropium combination by nebulizer every 20 minutes for 1 hour or continuously because this regimen is preferred to albuterol alone in severe asthma exacerbations treated in the ED (Answer B is correct). The regimens of either albuterol MDI alone or albuterol MDI plus OCS do not include the co

q: J.P. has a history of primary severe mitral regurgitation. J.P. is preparing to undergo valve replacement with a mechanical valve. J.P.'s medical history is significant for obesity and a provoked deep vein thrombosis (DVT). Current medications include liraglutide 3 mg subcutaneously daily. J.P. does not use tobacco or alcohol products. Vitals include a heart rate of 84 beats/minute and blood pressure is 120/74 mm Hg. J.P.'s SCr is 1.0 mg/dL, correlating with a CrCl of 120 mL/minute. All other laboratory values and diagnostics are within normal limits. Which is the optimal anticoagulant regimen for preventing thrombosis in J.P. after mechanical mitral valve replacement?
opts: A. Dose-adjust warfarin to INR goal of 2.0-3.0 (INR target 2.5)
B. Dose-adjust warfarin to INR goal of 2.5-3.5 (INR target 3.0)
C. Dose-adjust warfarin to INR goal of 2.0-3.0 for first 3 months, then target 1.5-2.0 with aspirin 81 mg orally daily
D. Dose-adjust warfarin to INR goal of 2.5-3.5 for first 3 months, then target 2.0-2.5 with aspirin 81 mg orally daily
exp: Given the patient's receipt of a mechanical valve in the mitral position regardless of the presence/absence of a thromboembolic risk factor (e.g., history of DVT), standard-intensity therapy to an INR target of 3.0 is indicated (Answer B is correct). Answer A would be correct if the patient were receiving a mechanical valve replacement in the aortic position and no thromboembolism risk factors were present (Answer A is incorrect). A low-intensity vitamin K antagonist with concomitant low-dose as

q: A 71-year-old woman has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. She smokes half a pack of cigarettes per day and is lactose intolerant with minimal intake of dairy products. She takes calcium 1200 mg orally per day in divided doses and vitamin D 600 international units/day orally. Her calculated creatinine clearance (CrCl) is 60-70 mL/minute. Her bone mineral density T-score is −2.6 at the hip and −2.1 at the spine. Her Fracture Risk Assessment Tool score indicates she has a 10-year probability of a major osteoporotic fracture of 22% and a 10-year probability of a hip fracture of 11%. Of the following, which is the best therapy to recommend?
opts: A. Continue calcium 1200 mg/vitamin D 600 international units orally daily
B. Add abaloparatide 80 mcg subcutaneously daily and continue calcium 1200 mg/vitamin D 600 international units orally daily
C. Add romosozumab 210 mg subcutaneously every month, continue calcium 1200 mg orally daily, and increase to vitamin D 800 international units orally daily
D. Add risedronate 35 mg orally every week, continue calcium 1200 mg orally daily, and increase to vitamin D 800 international units orally daily
exp: Drug therapy for osteoporosis is indicated for the following: a hip or vertebral fracture, a bone mineral density (BMD) T-score below −2.5 at the femoral neck or spine, or a BMD T-score between −1.0 and −2.5 at the femoral neck or spine and a 10-year probably of hip fracture 3% or greater or a 10-year probably of a major osteoporosis-related fracture of 20% or greater based on the Fracture Risk Assessment Tool (FRAX) system. This patient needs therapy because her T-score is less than −2.5 at the

q: A 72-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with chronic cough and dyspnea for the past 4 months. She denies dyspnea at rest and recent exacerbations of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) within the past year. Her COPD Assessment Test (CAT) score is 9. Which regimen is best to recommend as initial maintenance therapy for this patient's COPD?
opts: A. Fluticasone-salmeterol
B. Fluticasone furoate-umeclidinium-vilanterol
C. Tiotropium-olodaterol
D. Umeclidinium
exp: According to the Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease guidelines, this patient's asthma is classified as group A because she has not had any exacerbations within the past year and her CAT score is less than 10. A long-acting anticholinergic-muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) is preferred for group A (Answer D is correct). An inhaled corticosteroid (ICS)-long-acting β2-agonist (LABA) combination is not recommended in the treatment of COPD (Answer A is incorrect). A LAMA-LABA-ICS combi

q: A 24-year-old woman with a history of tonic-clonic seizures achieves symptom control with phenytoin 100 mg by mouth three times daily. Her serum phenytoin concentration is normally 13 mg/L. She is adherent to therapy and was recently prescribed carbamazepine, with the most recent laboratory values from a clinic visit showing a total serum phenytoin concentration of 6.5 mg/L and a free concentration of albumin of 2.5 g/dL. Given the current information, which changes would be best to suggest?
opts: A. Increase the phenytoin dose to 200 mg by mouth three times daily
B. Increase the phenytoin dose to 100 mg by mouth four times daily
C. Decrease the phenytoin dose to 100 mg by mouth twice daily
D. Maintain the current dosage
exp: The patient has hypoalbuminemia. Phenytoin concentrations should be corrected for low albumin. The patient's corrected phenytoin serum concentration is 8.4 mg/L, below the lower end of the therapeutic range (10-20 mg/L). The dosage should be increased (Answer B is correct). The dosage should be increased slowly because of phenytoin's nonlinear kinetics; doubling the dose may lead to excessive exposure and contribute to toxicity (Answer A is incorrect). Because the patient is not exhibiting seizu

q: A.B., an 82-year-old man, presents to the geriatrics office accompanied by his daughter, who is concerned about her father's increased "forgetfulness," inability to dress himself in the morning, and frequent coughing after eating. The patient's medical history is significant for hypertension, coronary artery disease, heart failure, and Parkinson disease (PD). He takes the following medications: carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 mg orally three times daily, lisinopril 20 mg orally daily, furosemide 20 mg orally daily, potassium chloride 20 mEq orally daily, and carvedilol 12.5 mg orally twice daily. His Mini-Mental State Examination score is 10/30. Blood tests and urinalysis were unremarkable. No nutritional deficiencies are noted. Current blood pressure is 114/72 mm Hg and pulse rate is 64 beats/min. The patient is diagnosed with dementia of advanced PD. Which therapeutic recommendation is best to initiate for A.B. at this time?
opts: A. Rivastigmine 4.6-mg transdermal patch daily
B. Pimavanserin 34 mg daily
C. Donepezil 10 mg daily
D. Memantine 10 mg daily
exp: Cholinesterase inhibitors (CIs) can be used in dementia of advanced PD, of which efficacy between agents does not significantly differ. The patient's coughing after eating may indicate dysphagia, which is common in advanced PD. Because of this, a transdermal CI like rivastigmine may be better tolerated (Answer A is correct). Currently, the patient has no signs attributable to PD psychosis, making pimavanserin inappropriate at this time (Answer B is incorrect). Although donepezil and memantine ar

q: A 41-year-old patient presents to the ED with a headache and severe neck pain. The patient reports that light and sound worsen the headache. A lumbar puncture is performed, and the CSF culture grows Streptococcus pneumoniae. Which prophylactic strategy is most recommended for household contacts?
opts: A. No prophylaxis is necessary for household adults or children; monitor for signs and symptoms consistent with meningitis
B. Adults should receive rifampin 600 mg orally daily; children should receive 20 mg/kg orally daily for 4 days
C. Adults should receive rifampin 600 mg orally twice daily; children should receive 10 mg/kg orally twice daily for 4 doses
D. Adults should receive the 20-valent pneumococcal vaccine; children should receive the 15-valent pneumococcal vaccine
exp: No prophylaxis is recommended for people exposed to a person with confirmed pneumococcal meningitis (Answer A is correct). Pneumococcal vaccines can be administered to close contacts if they meet CDC vaccine criteria, but vaccines do not offer acute protection for invasive pneumococcal disease (Answer D is incorrect). The daily dosing of rifampin is recommended for people exposed to Haemophilus influenzae meningitis, and twice-daily dosing is recommended for those exposed to Neisseria meningitid

q: A 49-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with flank pain and temperatures to 102.2 degreesF. She has no other symptoms. She is otherwise healthy and has received no antibiotics for the past few years. Her urinalysis is cloudy and shows white blood cell count 100-200 cells/mm3, many bacteria, red blood cell count 6-10 cells/mm3, and leukocyte esterase positive. Which best describes the patient's condition?
opts: A. The patient has a complicated cystitis
B. The patient has an uncomplicated pyelonephritis
C. The patient has a complicated pyelonephritis
D. The patient has asymptomatic bacteriuria and should not be treated with antibiotics
exp: The patient has a fever and flank pain. Therefore, she does not have asymptomatic bacteriuria (Answer D is incorrect). The fever and flank pain also mean that the infection is not located only in the bladder. Therefore, it is not a cystitis (Answer A is incorrect). Because of her age and lack of any comorbidities or recent antibiotics, this is not a complicated urinary tract infection (Answer C is incorrect). Thus, the patient has an uncomplicated pyelonephritis and should be treated with the ap

q: Ten days ago, a patient with diabetes and G3a, A3 chronic kidney disease had a baseline SCr of 1.4 mg/dL and was initiated on lisinopril to improve her proteinuria. Today, she returns with an SCr of 1.9 mg/dL and a persistent dry cough, which is keeping her awake at night. Which plan is most appropriate for this patient?
opts: A. Decrease lisinopril dose and monitor SCr in 1 week
B. Continue lisinopril and monitor SCr again in 2 weeks
C. Discontinue lisinopril and initiate losartan in 2 weeks if SCr is at baseline
D. Change lisinopril to losartan today and monitor SCr in 1 week
exp: This patient's SCr acutely increased by about 36%. An acute increase of greater than 30% in SCr after initiation is an indication to hold therapy (Answers B and D are incorrect). A newly developed cough that is affecting quality of life is an indication to change angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) to angiotensin receptor blocker (ARB) therapy (Answer A is incorrect). Because of the elevation in SCr, this should be done after holding therapy and allowing a return to baseline (Answer C is correct

q: A man with a history of asthma presents to the outpatient clinic because of worsening cough and shortness of breath for the past 3 days. He denies dyspnea at rest. He has not used his albuterol metered dose inhaler as needed for the past week because he has been unable to pick up his refill at the pharmacy. He reports continuing his maintenance inhaler during this time. He has no recent history of oral corticosteroid (OCS) use. His forced expiratory volume in the first second of expiration (FEV1) is 62% of predicted. The patient's oxygen saturation (room air) is 93%. Which is best to initially treat this patient's asthma exacerbation?
opts: A. Albuterol by nebulizer every 20 minutes for 1 hour
B. Albuterol and ipratropium by nebulizer every 20 minutes for 1 hour
C. Intravenous methylprednisolone
D. Oral prednisone
exp: The patient is presenting with a mild to moderate asthma exacerbation because of an FEV1 greater than 50% of predicted without dyspnea at rest. Recommended treatment of mild-moderate asthma exacerbations includes an inhaled short-acting β2-agonist (SABA) at increased frequency (Answer A is correct). Inhaled combination SABA and ipratropium by nebulizer is recommended specifically for severe asthma exacerbations (Answer B is incorrect). Intravenous corticosteroids are not recommended unless the p

q: A 55-year-old patient with a history of solid organ transplantation received the 15-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV15) 1 year ago. Which pneumococcal vaccine recommendation is best?
opts: A. Administer the 21-valent PCV (PCV21) at the visit today
B. Administer the 20-valent PCV (PCV20) at the visit today
C. Administer the 23-valent pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine (PPSV23) today
D. Administer the PCV20 when the patient turns 65
exp: The patient's history of solid organ transplantation is an indication for multiple pneumococcal doses between 19 and 64 years of age (Answer D is incorrect). Given that 1 dose of the PCV15 was received 1 year ago, the patient is a candidate for the PPSV23 as early as 8 weeks after receiving the PCV15 (Answer C is correct). Although the PCV20 and PCV21 can be administered 1 year after receiving the PCV15, they are only recommended if PPSV23 is not available (Answers A and B are incorrect).

q: A 36-year-old woman with no significant medical history presents to the emergency department with the "worst headache of my life."
opts: A. Nimodipine 60 mg orally every 4 hours
B. Recombinant activated factor VII 90 mcg/kg intravenously once
C. Levetiracetam 1000 mg intravenously every 12 hours
D. Clevidipine 2 mg/hour intravenously
exp: Achieving blood pressure control would be best for the acute management of her SAH to reduce the risk of rebleeding, and a clevidipine infusion can be titrated to achieve a goal systolic blood pressure less than 160 mm Hg (Answer D is correct). Nimodipine should be administered at some point to reduce the risk of delayed cerebral ischemia and improve neurologic outcomes, but it is not the most urgent intervention at this time (Answer A is incorrect). Recombinant activated factor VII does not imp

q: J.N. is an 82-year-old woman (height 157 cm, weight 49 kg) with a confirmed diagnosis of a deep vein thrombosis in the posterior tibial vein. Her medical history is significant for metastatic non-small cell lung cancer with lesions to the GI system, dyslipidemia, and depression. Her medication regimen is nivolumab, carboplatin, and paclitaxel infused intravenously every 3 weeks; pravastatin 40 mg orally daily; and sertraline 100 mg orally daily. Her SCr is 1.0 mg/dL with a correlating CrCl of 33 mL/min. All other laboratory values are within normal limits. Which is the most appropriate anticoagulant regimen to initiate for J.N.?
opts: A. Apixaban 2.5 mg orally twice daily
B. Enoxaparin 50 mg subcutaneously twice daily
C. Dabigatran 150 mg orally twice daily after 5 days of enoxaparin 60 mg subcutaneously twice daily
D. Dose-adjusted warfarin to INR of 2.0 to 3.0
exp: Venous thromboembolism treatment strategy is higher-intensity apixaban at 10 mg orally twice daily for 7 days, then standard dosing of 5 mg orally twice daily thereafter. The described apixaban dosing would be for a patient with nonvalvular atrial fibrillation because of age older than 80 and weight less than 60 kg (Answer A is incorrect). Dabigatran has limited evidence to support effective treatment in active cancer compared with low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH). Therefore, guidelines do no

q: A 35-year-old otherwise healthy patient presents with a 3-day history of cough, fever, and malaise. A chest radiograph confirms community-acquired pneumonia (CAP). The patient has no significant comorbidities, no recent antibiotic use, and no known drug allergies. Local pneumococcal macrolide resistance is 20%. Which empiric outpatient antibiotic regimen is most appropriate for this patient?
opts: A. Azithromycin 500 mg on day 1, then 250 mg daily for 4 days
B. Levofloxacin 750 mg orally daily for 5 days
C. Amoxicillin-clavulanic acid (Augmentin) 875 mg/125 mg orally twice daily for 7 days
D. Cefpodoxime 200 mg orally twice daily for 7 days plus azithromycin 500 mg daily for 3 days
exp: For outpatients with CAP who are otherwise healthy and have no risk factors for drug-resistant Streptococcus pneumoniae, the 2019 Infectious Diseases Society of America/American Thoracic Society guidelines recommend macrolide monotherapy (azithromycin or clarithromycin) if local pneumococcal resistance is less than 25% (Answer A is correct). Answer B (levofloxacin) is incorrect because fluoroquinolones should be reserved for patients with comorbidities or risk factors for drug-resistant pathogen

q: A 53-year-old woman has a chief concern of abdominal pain that she describes as cramping, dull, and aching. Her medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, irritable bowel syndrome-diarrhea (IBS-D), and gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). Her home medications include hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily, loperamide 2 mg four times daily as needed for diarrhea, and famotidine 10 mg twice daily as needed for reflux. She states that she has been experiencing abdominal pain despite her diarrhea and GERD being well controlled with her current medication regimen. On further questioning, the patient reports decreased interest in daily activities, insomnia, and fatigue over the past 6 months as her abdominal pain has worsened. Which would be best to initiate to address the patient's current concerns?
opts: A. Sertraline
B. Cyclobenzaprine
C. Nortriptyline
D. Tramadol
exp: Nortriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant that has efficacy in treating neuropathic and GI pain related to IBS and can also decrease the patient's depressed mood (Answer C is correct). Sertraline is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor that may aid in the patient's depression, but its effects on muting visceral sensation in IBS are minimal (Answer A is incorrect). Tramadol is a partial opioid that may address the patient's abdominal pain, but it could become constipating and cause GI distr

q: A 30-year-old woman with chronic back pain is treating the pain with high-dose ibuprofen 800 mg orally three times daily and oxycodone 20 mg orally twice daily. She recently broke her tibia in a biking accident and was prescribed morphine immediate release for leg pain and docusate 200 mg as needed for constipation. Four days after the fracture, she has not had a bowel movement and has concerns of constipation. Her fracture pain is severe, and she anticipates needing morphine for 2 weeks before she can reduce her use of opioids. Which is best to recommend for this patient?
opts: A. Change docusate to 200 mg at bedtime on a scheduled basis
B. Add senna 2 tablets at bedtime on a scheduled basis
C. Add methylnaltrexone 12 mg subcutaneously every morning
D. Add a daily fiber supplement starting with 1 scoop daily and titrating as tolerated
exp: Opioid-induced constipation (OIC) is related to the activation of mu-opioid receptors in the GI tract. This results in a reduction in smooth muscle contractions and altered GI transit time. A stool softener like docusate is insufficient to resolve OIC (Answer A is incorrect). A stimulant laxative such as senna taken on a scheduled basis will increase smooth muscle activity in the GI tract and help resolve OIC (Answer B is correct). Methylnaltrexone is a peripherally acting mu-opioid receptor ant

q: Which supportive care measure must be met prior to initiation of therapeutic paralysis in a critically ill patient?
opts: A. Pain control to a Critical-Care Pain Observation Tool score of less than 3
B. Efficacy trial with boluses of selected neuromuscular blocker
C. Sedation control to a Richmond Agitation-Sedation Score of 0 to −2
D. Baseline train-of-four score of 1 of 4 twitches
exp: Adequate pain and sedation must be achieved prior to initiating therapeutic paralysis. Patients must be mechanically ventilated with adequate pain control (evidenced by Critical-Care Pain Observation Tool score of less than 3) and deep sedation (evidenced by Richmond Agitation-Sedation Score [RASS] −4 to −5). (Answer A is correct; Answer C is incorrect). An efficacy trial is not recommended prior to therapeutic paralysis; intravenous bolus doses of the selected neuromuscular blocker are routinel

q: A 22-year-old woman (height 163 cm, weight 58 kg) is admitted to the intensive care unit for hypoxemic respiratory failure secondary to acute respiratory distress syndrome and has been intubated and is receiving cisatracurium therapeutic paralysis for 2 days, in addition to fentanyl and propofol infusions. The hypoxemia is now resolved, cisatracurium has been weaned off, and fentanyl and propofol are currently off for spontaneous breathing and awakening trials as extubation is being planned. The intensivist would like to start nutrition today if extubation is successful. Which is the best approach to determine the patient's recommended nutritional needs?
opts: A. Initiate supplemental parenteral nutrition until patient meets goal of 725 kcal/day and 35 grams/day protein
B. Increase propofol continuous infusion rate to provide a higher amount of fat-based calories
C. Conduct indirect calorimetry to determine percentage of total calories to be supplemented daily
D. Target calories 1450 kcal/day and target protein 70 grams/day
exp: The 2022 ASPEN guidelines recommend for critically ill non-obese adults, caloric estimates of 12-25 kcal/kg/day and protein estimates of 1.2-2 grams/kg/day are recommended. This patient is not obese, and thus is estimated to require 25 kcal/kg/day (~ 1450 kcal/day) and 1.2 grams/kg/day protein (~70 grams/day) (Answer D is correct). Supplemental parenteral nutrition is not recommended for initiation of nutrition. Studies have assessed supplemental parenteral nutrition in patients who cannot meet

q: L.V. is a 55-year-old patient (height 180 cm, weight 92 kg) who has a mechanical heart valve in the aortic position. L.V. takes warfarin 5 mg orally daily except for 2.5 mg on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays. The patient presents to the emergency department for management of a chronic obstructive pulmonary disease exacerbation. On laboratory evaluation, the patient's INR is 10.1, Hgb is 14.3 g/dL, Hct is 43%, and Plt is 145,000/mm3. The CBC results are consistent with baseline values. L.V.'s blood pressure is 130/78 mm Hg and heart rate is 89 beats/minute. There is no evidence (signs or symptoms) of bleeding. Which is best for managing L.V.'s elevated INR?
opts: A. Hold warfarin only
B. Hold warfarin and administer vitamin K 5 mg intravenously
C. Hold warfarin and administer vitamin K 5 mg orally
D. Hold warfarin and administer 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) 50 units/kg intravenously
exp: According to the practice guidelines, in patients with an elevated INR greater than 10 with no evidence of bleeding, warfarin should be discontinued, and vitamin K should be administered orally (Answer C is correct). Although the dosages of vitamin K 5 mg and 4PCC 50 units/kg administered intravenously are accurate for the reversal of warfarin, these agents are not indicated without evidence of a major or life-threatening bleed (Answers B and D are incorrect). Because of the patient's elevated I

q: A 73-year-old man is evaluated for reduced libido and erectile dysfunction. He has a 20-year history of diabetes, currently managed with metformin 1000 mg twice daily and empagliflozin (Jardiance) 25 mg daily. His most recent hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) is 7.3%. His testosterone concentration is 410 ng/dL (normal 240-950 ng/dL). His other laboratory values are within normal limits. He has not previously tried medications to improve his symptoms. Which is most recommended for this patient?
opts: A. Optimization of diabetes therapy
B. Testosterone
C. Tadalafil
D. Alprostadil
exp: Although uncontrolled diabetes may contribute to decreased libido and erectile dysfunction, this patient's diabetes is reasonably controlled, given his recent HbA1c value (Answer A is incorrect). This patient's testosterone concentrations are within normal limits, and testosterone replacement is only recommended when low concentrations are identified; testosterone monotherapy is not effective for erectile dysfunction (Answer B is incorrect). A phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) inhibitor is recomme

q: A.C. is an 11-year-old girl. While at school, her teacher notices her being distracted, staring off, almost "daydreaming" in class. She has noticed this consistently over the past 2 months. The teacher discusses this with her parents. The patient is diagnosed with a seizure disorder. Which medication is best to initiate for this patient?
opts: A. Topiramate
B. Carbamazepine
C. Levetiracetam
D. Ethosuximide
exp: Ethosuximide is the best option provided because it is a first-line treatment for absence seizures (Answer D is correct). Carbamazepine is not indicated for absence seizures and may make them worse (Answer B is incorrect). Topiramate is a third-line option and not the best option of the given choices (Answer A is incorrect). Levetiracetam is a fourth-line option and not the best of the given options (Answer C is incorrect).

q: A patient asks what lifestyle modifications they can make to reduce their blood pressure. Which intervention would result in the most significant blood pressure reduction?
opts: A. Smoking cessation
B. Abstaining from alcohol consumption
C. Adopting a diet low in potassium
D. Reducing weight by 15 kg
exp: Weight loss of 15 kg would result in a systolic blood pressure (SBP) reduction of 7-30 mm Hg (Answer D is correct). Answer C is not expected to result in blood pressure reduction. In fact, a diet rich in potassium-containing fruits and vegetables is an appropriate recommendation for reducing blood pressure (Answer C is incorrect). Answer B would result in SBP reduction, but the anticipated magnitude of effect is 2-4 mm Hg (Answer B is incorrect). Smoking cessation is an important recommendation

q: J.R. is a 63-year-old patient (height 157 cm, weight 80 kg) who presents to the emergency department with concerns of chest pain, shortness of breath, and palpitations. On examination, the patient's heart rate is 110 beats/minute and blood pressure is 140/92 mm Hg. J.R.'s D-dimer is positive, and CT pulmonary angiography reveals evidence of an acute pulmonary embolism. The patient's medical history is significant for chronic kidney disease, osteoporosis, HIV, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and hypertension. Current medications include denosumab 60 mg subcutaneously every 6 months, dolutegravir 50 mg orally daily, darunavir 800 mg orally daily, cobicistat 150 mg orally daily, tiotropium 2.5 mcg/olodaterol 2.5 mcg per actuation - 2 inhalations once daily, and losartan 100 mg orally daily. Current labs reveal a SCr of 1.5 mg/dL and CrCl is 48 mL/minute. All other laboratory values are stable and within normal limits. Which is the most appropriate venous thromboembolism (VTE) treatment strategy to initiate for J.R.?
opts: A. Apixaban 5 mg orally twice daily for 7 days, followed by 2.5 mg orally twice daily thereafter
B. Dabigatran 75 mg orally twice daily after 5 days of parenteral heparin administered intravenously
C. Edoxaban 60 mg orally twice daily after 10 days of therapeutic parenteral heparin administered intravenously
D. Rivaroxaban 15 mg orally twice daily for 21 days, followed by 20 mg orally once daily thereafter
exp: Dabigatran is a reasonable direct oral anticoagulant in the treatment of VTE; however, the FDA-approved dosage is 150 mg orally twice daily after a short course of a parenteral anticoagulant (Answer B is incorrect). Edoxaban is recommended for use in VTE treatment; however, it requires a renal dose adjustment to 30 mg orally daily when the CrCl is 15-50 mL/minute, such as in this patient (Answer C is incorrect). Although the rivaroxaban regimen in Answer D is the standard regimen for VTE treatme

q: A 64-year-old man (180 cm, 80 kg) is currently receiving treatment for small cell lung cancer with metastatic disease to his spine. He is in the clinic today for his next cycle of therapy, and his family reports he has had episodes of confusion and fatigue over the past several days. Current laboratory results are as follows: absolute neutrophil count 2400 cells/mm3, albumin 2.6 g/dL, HCO3 24 mmol/L, serum urea nitrogen 31 mg/dL, total serum calcium 13.2 mg/dL, Cl 102 mEq/L, glucose 98 mg/dL, Hct 27.6%, Hgb 9.2 g/dL, Plt 180,000/mm3, K 3.5 mEq/L, SCr 1.1 mg/dL, Na 143 mEq/L, and WBC 4.6 × 103 cells/mm3. Which recommendation is best for the patient at this time?
opts: A. Initiate fluid hydration with 1 L of normal saline over 3 hours; initiate calcitonin 8 units/kg intramuscularly for one dose plus zoledronic acid 4 mg intravenously for one dose
B. Initiate fluid hydration with 1 L of normal saline over 3 hours; initiate calcitonin 200 units/kg administered as 1 spray in each nostril once daily
C. Initiate fluid hydration with 1 L of dextrose 5% in water over 3 hours; initiate denosumab 120 subcutaneously for one dose
D. Initiate fluid hydration with 1 L of dextrose 5% in water over 3 hours; initiate zoledronic acid 4 mg for one dose
exp: Given the corrected calcium of greater than 14 mg/dL and CNS symptoms, this patient is currently experiencing severe and symptomatic hypercalcemia of malignancy. He needs immediate aggressive treatment and management, likely with inpatient admission. This condition is considered an oncologic emergency. The proper treatment is hydration with normal saline (not dextrose 5% in water) and denosumab or intravenous bisphosphonate therapy together with intramuscular calcitonin therapy for rapid reversa

q: A 41-year-old woman presents to the clinic with concerns of recent episodes of what she believes may be seizures. She has a medical history of immunoglobulin A nephropathy, end-stage renal disease (hemodialysis three times weekly), hypertension, and hypothyroidism. The patient is initiated on phenytoin and is receiving phenytoin 600 mg orally daily in the evening. A trough phenytoin concentration is 17.2 mg/L. Which best assesses her phenytoin therapy?
opts: A. Her concentration is in the therapeutic range and should not be adjusted
B. Her concentration is in the therapeutic range, but she should receive 300 mg in the morning and 300 mg in the evening
C. Her concentration is too low, and she should take an extra dose on days of dialysis
D. Her concentration is too high, and her dose should be lowered significantly
exp: Although the patient's total phenytoin concentration is in the therapeutic range (10-20 mg/L), her concentration, when adjusted for her chronic renal failure, is 34.4 mg/L and too high (Answer A is incorrect). Although phenytoin doses above 400 mg should be split up, adjusted concentration is well above the therapeutic range, and the total daily dose should be decreased (Answer B is incorrect). Phenytoin is not eliminated by hemodialysis, and extra doses after dialysis are not needed. In additio

q: A patient is receiving a drug that follows linear pharmacokinetics, and the volume of distribution goes from 1 L/kg to 0.7 L/kg. What is the best prediction to make based on this change?
opts: A. Concentration at steady state will be decreased
B. Time to steady state will be decreased
C. Amount of a loading dose will need to be increased
D. Clearance of the drug will be increased
exp: When the volume of distribution is decreased, the elimination rate constant is increased according to the equation k = Cl/Vd. When the elimination rate constant is increased, the half-life is decreased according to the equation t1/2 = 0.693/k. If the half-life is decreased, the time to steady state is decreased (Answer B is correct). The concentration at steady state is only a function of the dose and clearance, not of the volume of distribution (Vd) (Answer A is incorrect). Because the amount o
